docs.title: Session 27 — 3-Way Merge Viewer Architecture Design
docs.text: Status: Pre-implementation design (architect-only, read-only) Author: boomerang-architect Date: 2026-06-06 References: T-083 (P2 roadmap §227-261), T-030 (existing diff.ts, 710 lines)

docs.title: 1. Pane Layout Geometry
docs.text:
Three panes render side-by-side with borders. Base pane is read-only and visually dimmed; ours/theirs are selectable with highlight border.

┌─────────────────────── Three-Way Merge ────────────────────────┐
│ ┌── Base (original) ──┬──── Ours (local) ──┬── Theirs (proposed) ──┐ │
│ │                     │                    │                        │ │
│ │ (dimmed, read-only) │ ←ACTIVE→ (default) │   (selectable)         │ │
│ │                     │                    │                        │ │
│ └─────────────────────┴────────────────────┴────────────────────────┘ │
│ ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── │
│ [Tab] cycle  [1] base  [2] ours  [3] theirs  [y] accept  [n] reject  │
│ [q/Esc] quit without accepting                                        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Width Algorithm
docs.text:
Terminal width >= 80 chars; target layout >= 60 chars per pane group.

outer_border    = 2          (│ left + │ right)
inner_separators = 2 × 1     (│ between panes)  = 2
total_border_overhead         = 4
content_width = terminal.width - total_border_overhead

base_w   = max(15, floor(content_width × 0.25))  // 25% — smallest, dimmed
ours_w   = floor((content_width - base_w) / 2)   // split remaining evenly
theirs_w = content_width - base_w - ours_w        // ensure all pixels used
Terminal total_border content base_w ours_w theirs_w 80 cols 4 76 19 28 29 100 cols 4 96 24 36 36 120 cols 4 116 29 43 44

Minimum terminal: 60 columns. Below 60, fall back to vertical stack (not in scope — raises an error with message "Terminal too narrow for 3-way view (min 60 cols)").

docs.title: Pane Visual States
docs.text:
Pane Default border Active border Text rendering base dim gray dim gray (no change) dimmed (fg: #888) ours white/cyan cyan bold (active highlight) normal theirs white/cyan cyan bold (active highlight) normal

Base pane ignores selection — it is always read-only display. Line numbers gutter: " 123 " (5 chars) on each pane, subtract from content width.

docs.title: 2.1 DiffMode Enum (additive to existing code)
docs.text:
export type DiffMode = "twoWay" | "threeWay";

DiffPanel stores _mode: DiffMode = "twoWay". The existing show() sets "twoWay"; new showThreeWay() sets "threeWay".

docs.title: 2.2 ThreeWayState (new, additive)
docs.text:
export type ThreeWayState =
  | "idle"           // panel hidden / not shown
  | "ours-active"    // ours pane has cursor; y=accept ours
  | "theirs-active"  // theirs pane has cursor; y=accept theirs
  | "base-active"    // base pane selected (read-only; y is no-op)
  | "accepted"       // merged with active pane's version
  | "rejected"       // discarded theirs, kept ours
  | "error";         // rendering failure or invalid input

docs.title: 2.3 Transition Diagram
docs.text:
showThreeWay()
                      │
          ┌───────────▼────────────┐
          │      ours-active       │◄────────── [default on show]
          │  (ours highlighted)    │
          └──┬──────────┬────┬─────┘
             │ Tab      │ 1  │ 2
    ┌────────▼───────┐  │    │
    │  theirs-active │◄─┘    │
    │ (theirs highlit)│      │
    └──┬────┬────┬───┘      │
       │Tab │ 1  │ 3        │
  ┌────▼────┴──┐ │           │
  │ base-active│◄┘───────────┘
  │(base dimmed,│
  │ read-only)  │
  └──┬────┬─────┘
     │Tab │ 2/3
     │    └──► ours-active / theirs-active
     │
     ├── y ─────► accepted   (ours-active → accept ours; theirs-active → accept theirs)
     │                ⚠ base-active + y → no-op (beep or status: "Base is read-only")
     │
     └── n ─────► rejected   (any active state → "discard theirs, keep ours")
                    q/Esc ──► idle        (any active state → hide panel)

     idle         → ours-active  (showThreeWay called again)

     accepted     → idle         (q/Esc → hide)
     rejected     → idle         (q/Esc → hide)
     error        → idle         (q/Esc → hide)

Critical invariant: ours-active is the ONLY state where ours is the active pane. theirs-active is the ONLY state where theirs is the active pane. base-active is informational — y does nothing, n still rejects.

docs.title: 2.4 Existing 2-Way State Machine (UNTOUCHED)
docs.text:
hidden → showing → accepted / rejected / blocked

The _mode = "twoWay" path uses the ORIGINAL DiffPanelState machine with zero changes. The 3-way state machine runs in a SEPARATE _threeWayState field and only when _mode === "threeWay". handleKey() dispatches on _mode at the top level:

async handleKey(keyName: string): Promise<boolean> {
  if (this._mode === "threeWay") return this._handleThreeWayKey(keyName);
  return this._handleTwoWayKey(keyName); // existing code, renamed
}

docs.title: 3.1 Key Map
docs.text:
Key ours-active theirs-active base-active accepted/rejected/error Tab → theirs-active → base-active → ours-active consumed (no-op) 1 → base-active → base-active stays base-active consumed (no-op) 2 stays ours-active → ours-active → ours-active consumed (no-op) 3 → theirs-active stays theirs-active → theirs-active consumed (no-op) y → accepted (ours) → accepted (theirs) no-op + status: "read-only" consumed (no-op) n → rejected → rejected → rejected consumed (no-op) q → idle (hide) → idle (hide) → idle (hide) → idle (hide) Esc → idle (hide) → idle (hide) → idle (hide) → idle (hide)

All unrecognized keys: consumed (prevent leaking to chat input). Panel is modal.

docs.title: 3.2 2-Way Key Map (EXISTING, UNCHANGED)
docs.text:
y → accept    n → reject    q/Esc → hide

No Tab or numeric keys in 2-way mode — these are no-ops (consumed by panel but ignored).

docs.title: 4.1 Entry Points (additive, no rewrite)
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────┐
                    │      DiffPanel        │
                    │  _mode: DiffMode      │
                    │  _threeWayState       │
                    │  _activePaneIdx       │
                    │  _threeWayInput       │
                    ├──────────────────────┤
  twoWay path ───►  │  show()         ◄── existing entry point (T-030)
                    │  handleKey()          │
                    │  _handleTwoWayKey()   │ ← renamed from _handleKey logic
                    ├──────────────────────┤
  threeWay path ──► │  showThreeWay()  ◄── NEW entry point (T-083)
                    │  _handleThreeWayKey() │ ← new key handler with pane routing
                    ├──────────────────────┤
  shared ─────────► │  renderDiffPanel()   │ ← extended with mode param
                    │  _updateDisplay()     │ ← extended for 3-way layout
                    │  createRenderable()   │ ← shared box (visible toggle)
                    └──────────────────────┘

docs.title: 4.2 renderDiffPanel Signature (extended)
docs.text:
export function renderDiffPanel(
  input: DiffInput,
  title?: string,
  options?: { mode?: DiffMode; activePaneIdx?: 0 | 1 | 2 },
): DiffPanelRenderResult;
  • When mode === "threeWay", calls renderThreeWayLayout(input, options) internally
  • When mode === "twoWay" or absent, runs EXISTING code path (unchanged)
  • Returns same DiffPanelRenderResult shape (lines, additions, removals, state, statusMessage)

docs.title: 4.3 What the Coder MUST NOT Touch
docs.text: Do NOT modify Because DiffPanelState type 2-way state machine must remain untouched show() method body Rename internals to _handleTwoWayKey() but keep logic identical Confidence / TestResult types These are 2-way concepts; 3-way has no test callback DiffInput interface 3-way uses a SEPARATE ThreeWayDiffInput, not an extension createRenderable() box/Text IDs Reuse "diff-panel", "diff-content", "diff-status" IDs getWrapUpEvidence() Return null in 3-way mode (no wrap-up evidence for merge viewer)

docs.title: 5. Data Model
docs.text:
/** Input for the 3-way merge viewer. */
export interface ThreeWayDiffInput {
  /** Original file content before any changes (ancestor). */
  base: string;
  /** Local/current file content (our version). */
  ours: string;
  /** Proposed/coder's file content (their version). */
  theirs: string;
  /** Optional conflict marker lines from a git merge (e.g., "<<<<<<< ours"). */
  conflictMarkers?: string[];
}

/** Stores a single pane's rendered output. */
interface ThreeWayPaneData {
  label: "base" | "ours" | "theirs";
  lines: string[];
  additions: number;
  removals: number;
  isReadOnly: boolean;
  isActive: boolean;
}

The DiffPanel stores _threeWayPanes: ThreeWayPaneData[] internally. Panes are computed on showThreeWay() by running generateDiffFromBeforeAfter() three times: - Pane 0: base (diff against empty) — renders full file content - Pane 1: ours (diff base→ours) — renders what changed locally - Pane 2: theirs (diff base→theirs) — renders what the coder proposes

Alternatively (simpler and preferred for v0.2.0): - Each pane renders the FULL file content directly (not as diffs), one line per pane row - Align lines across panes by line number (scroll synced) - This simplifies rendering and avoids 3-way diff computation complexity

Decision: pure text rendering per pane — each pane renders content.split("\n") directly with line numbers. No diff computation between panes in v0.2.0. Conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>) are displayed as-is if present.

docs.title: 6. Acceptance Criteria (for coder implementation, T-083)
docs.text:
1. showThreeWay() renders 3 panes — calling panel.showThreeWay({ base, ours, theirs }) with 80-col terminal produces 3 visible panes with base (left, dimmed), ours (center, highlighted), theirs (right, normal) and a keybinding bar at bottom.

  2. Tab cycles correctly — Tab press in any order produces: ours-active → theirs-active → base-active → ours-active. The highlight border moves to the correct pane on each press.

  3. Numeric jumps (½/3) direct — Pressing 1 activates base (read-only, dimmed), 2 activates ours, 3 activates theirs, regardless of current active pane. Edge case: pressing the same number twice keeps the pane active (no flicker).

  4. Accept with active pane — y in ours-active state accepts ours (panel state → accepted, status: "✓ Merged with ours"). y in theirs-active accepts theirs (status: "✓ Merged with theirs"). y in base-active does NOTHING (status: "Base pane is read-only — select ours or theirs to accept").

  5. Reject discards theirs — n from any active state transitions to rejected (status: "✗ Merge rejected — keeping ours"). This is the same behavior regardless of which pane is active.

  6. 2-way mode zero regression — Call panel.show({ diff: "..." }) (or any existing T-030 path) with _mode = "twoWay". y/n/q/Esc produce the EXACT same behavior as before this change. 2-way handleKey returns identical state transitions. All existing diff tests pass without modification.

  7. Narrow terminal graceful degradation — Terminal width < 60 displays an error box: "Terminal too narrow for 3-way view (min 60 cols)" instead of a broken layout. The panel does NOT render garbled or overlapping text.

  8. All keys consumed when panel active — When 3-way panel is visible, ALL key presses (including unrecognized ones) are consumed by handleKey and do NOT leak to the underlying chat input. Modal integrity maintained.

  9. bun test + bun run typecheck — 12 new 3-way tests pass, 0 2-way regressions, 0 type errors.

docs.title: 7. Risk Mitigation — What the Coder MUST NOT Do
docs.text:
1. DO NOT modify DiffPanelState — the 5-state 2-way machine (hidden | showing | accepted | rejected | blocked) is sacrosanct. Adding a new state like threeWayActive to the existing union type would break the 2-way handleKey switch-case. Create a SEPARATE ThreeWayState type and a _threeWayState field.

  2. DO NOT change the show() method signature — existing callers (/diff command, kanban wrap-up) pass DiffInput. Keep the old signature and add showThreeWay() as a separate method. Do not add mode to show() — use the _mode field as internal dispatch.

  3. DO NOT touch DiffInput interface — 3-way has different data shape (3 strings vs. 1 diff). Create ThreeWayDiffInput. Do not cram base/ours/theirs fields into DiffInput.

  4. DO NOT render 3 panes as 3 separate diff runs — computing pairwise diffs (base→ours, base→theirs, ours→theirs) would balloon complexity. Render each pane as raw file content with line numbers. Pure text, no diff engine.

  5. DO NOT add TestResult or confidence gates to 3-way — the merge viewer is a visual comparison tool, not a verification pipeline. _acceptCallback and confidence logic (low → blocked) are 2-way concepts. 3-way y transitions directly to accepted/rejected without running tests.

  6. DO NOT inline the 3-way key handler into the existing handleKey switch-case — create _handleThreeWayKey() as a separate method. The existing handler should be renamed _handleTwoWayKey() and called via a top-level mode dispatch. This keeps the two state machines visually separate in the source.

End of design. Handoff to coder for T-083 implementation.

docs.title: Neuralgentics v0.2.0 — Docker/Podman Container Stack
docs.text: Status: Design complete (Architect phase) Target: Coder Context Package for T-FOLLOWUP-CONTAINERS-ENABLE Date: 2026-06-06 Branch: feat/containers (to be created by coder)

docs.title: What We're Shipping in v0.2.0
docs.text:
Four working container images, built and pushed to GHCR by CI, pullable via docker-compose up or podman-compose up:

Image GHCR Tag Purpose ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-postgres v0.2.0 PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector extension ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-sidecar v0.2.0 Python gRPC embedding service ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-backend v0.2.0 Go JSON-RPC backend (distroless) ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-tui v0.2.0 Terminal UI (compiled Bun binary, distroless)

Both docker-compose up and podman-compose up work end-to-end: - Postgres with schema auto-applied on first start. - Sidecar loads the embedding model on first startup (persisted via named volume). - Backend connects to postgres + sidecar, exposes JSON-RPC over stdio. - TUI connects to backend for interactive use.

docs.title: Why Now
docs.text:
The current install path requires 5 dependencies: 1. Go toolchain (for building the backend) 2. Node/Bun (for building the TUI) 3. Podman or Docker (for running the sidecar) 4. SSL certificates (manual setup for TLS) 5. Manual sidecar startup

podman-compose up reduces this to 1 dependency (Podman or Docker). Users pull images from GHCR and run a single command.

docs.title: What This Is NOT
docs.text:
• NOT a production Kubernetes deployment.
  • NOT a multi-node / high-availability setup.
  • NOT a scaling story (single postgres, single sidecar, single backend).
  • NOT an SSL/TLS-enabled deployment (containers communicate on internal bridge network; no encryption needed).

docs.title: PostgreSQL + pgvector: pgvector/pgvector:pg18
docs.text:
User-locked decision from Session 28. Not revisitable.

  • Rationale: The official pgvector image keeps postgres + pgvector extension in lockstep. pg18 is the current stable major version. Upgrading now avoids tech debt down the road (pg17 EOL).
  • How schema gets baked in: PostgreSQL's Docker entrypoint auto-executes any .sql or .sh files found in /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/ on first container start, in alphabetical order. We copy all *.up.sql migration files into that directory. The entrypoint runs them against POSTGRES_DB.
  • SHA pin decision: DROP the SHA digest pin. The current postgres.Dockerfile pins @sha256:8a6a4e93... (a pg17 digest). For the rewrite, use pgvector/pgvector:pg18 (tag-only). Official Docker Hub images are rarely retagged maliciously; the operational burden of updating SHA pins on every minor release outweighs the supply-chain benefit for a single-node local install. If the team later needs strict reproducibility (e.g., regulated environments), the SHA can be re-added by commenting # Pin with: podman inspect pgvector/pgvector:pg18 --format='{{.RepoDigests}}'.
  • Existing Dockerfile reference: docker/postgres.Dockerfile (12 lines, current). Lines 2–4 contain the old base + SHA.

docs.title: Sidecar: python:3.11-slim-bookworm
docs.text: Unchanged from current. The existing docker/sidecar.Dockerfile already uses multi-stage with this base. Python 3.11 is the LTS version with the broadest ML library support. slim-bookworm is smaller than full Debian but has the C toolchain needed for grpcio native extensions during the build stage.

docs.title: Backend: gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
docs.text: Unchanged. The Go binary is statically linked (CGO_ENABLED=0), so a shell-less distroless image is ideal: minimal attack surface, no package manager, no shell injection risk. ca-certificates are copied from the builder for TLS connections.

docs.title: TUI: gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
docs.text: Unchanged. bun build --compile produces a standalone binary, so distroless is sufficient.

docs.title: postgres.Dockerfile (single-stage)
docs.text:
FROM pgvector/pgvector:pg18

COPY packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/store/migrations/postgres/*.up.sql \
     /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/

ENV POSTGRES_DB=neuralgentics

EXPOSE 5432

HEALTHCHECK --interval=5s --timeout=3s --start-period=10s --retries=10 \
    CMD pg_isready -U postgres -d neuralgentics

Changes from current (v0.1.0): - Base image: pgvector/pgvector:pg17@sha256:... → pgvector/pgvector:pg18 - Added explicit EXPOSE 5432 - Added inline HEALTHCHECK directive (currently the healthcheck is only in docker-compose.yml) - Dropped SHA digest pin (rationale in Section 2)

Migration files copied (4 files, executed in order): 1. 000001_initial_schema.up.sql — core memories, peers, knowledge graph tables 2. 000002_project_chunks.up.sql — project indexing tables 3. 000003_memories_1024.up.sql — dual-model 1024-dim embedding table 4. 000004_agent_tools.up.sql — agent tool registration

docs.title: sidecar.Dockerfile (2-stage: builder + slim)
docs.text:
Stage 1 — Builder: 

FROM python:3.11-slim-bookworm AS builder

RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends build-essential && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN pip install --no-cache-dir uv

WORKDIR /app

COPY packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/requirements.txt .
RUN uv pip install --system --no-cache -r requirements.txt

Stage 2 — Final: 

FROM python:3.11-slim-bookworm

COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/

WORKDIR /app

COPY packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/main.py .
COPY packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/embedding_sidecar/ embedding_sidecar/
COPY packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/proto/ proto/

EXPOSE 50051

ENV PYTHONPATH=/app
ENV NEURAL_EMBED_ADDR=0.0.0.0:50051

HEALTHCHECK --interval=10s --timeout=7s --start-period=30s --retries=5 \
    CMD python -c "import grpc; ch=grpc.insecure_channel('localhost:50051'); grpc.channel_ready_future(ch).result(timeout=5)" || exit 1

CMD ["python", "main.py"]

Changes from current (v0.1.0): - Dropped SHA digest pins on both FROM lines (tag-only) - No functional changes — the existing structure is already correct

Result size: The builder includes build-essential (~200 MB) for compiling native extensions (grpcio, sentence-transformers). The final image is ~400-600 MB (most of which is the Sentence-Transformers model downloaded at runtime). The 2-stage split prevents build tools from bloating the final image.

docs.title: backend.Dockerfile (2-stage: golang builder + distroless)
docs.text:
Stage 1 — Builder: 

FROM golang:1.24-bookworm AS builder

WORKDIR /src

COPY go.work go.work.sum ./

COPY packages/memory/ packages/memory/
COPY packages/orchestrator-go/ packages/orchestrator-go/
COPY packages/broker-go/ packages/broker-go/
COPY packages/backend-go/ packages/backend-go/

WORKDIR /src/packages/backend-go
RUN go mod download

ARG VERSION=v0.2.0
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build \
    -ldflags="-s -w -X main.version=${VERSION}" \
    -o /app/neuralgentics-backend ./cmd/backend/

Stage 2 — Final: 

FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot

COPY --from=builder /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt /etc/ssl/certs/

COPY --from=builder /app/neuralgentics-backend /usr/local/bin/neuralgentics-backend

EXPOSE 8080

ENTRYPOINT ["neuralgentics-backend"]

Changes from current (v0.1.0): - Dropped SHA digest pins on both FROM lines - Default VERSION arg bumped to v0.2.0 - No functional changes

Binary properties: CGO_ENABLED=0 produces a fully static binary. -ldflags="-s -w" strips debug symbols and the DWARF table, reducing binary size ~30%. -X main.version=${VERSION} injects the release tag for version reporting.

docs.title: tui.Dockerfile (2-stage: bun builder + distroless)
docs.text:
Stage 1 — Builder: 

FROM oven/bun:1.3-slim AS builder

WORKDIR /app

COPY packages/tui/package.json packages/tui/bun.lock ./
RUN bun install --frozen-lockfile

COPY packages/tui/src/ src/
COPY packages/tui/tsconfig.json ./

ARG VERSION=v0.2.0
RUN bun build --compile \
    --target=bun-linux-x64 \
    --outfile=dist/neuralgentics \
    src/index.ts

Stage 2 — Final: 

FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot

COPY --from=builder /app/dist/neuralgentics /usr/local/bin/neuralgentics

ENTRYPOINT ["neuralgentics"]

Changes from current (v0.1.0): - Dropped SHA digest pins on both FROM lines - Default VERSION arg bumped to v0.2.0 - No functional changes

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
1. Host port 6000 → postgres container port 5432. Per AGENTS.md constraint: "Port 6000 only." This is the sole externally exposed port.
  2. Internal services communicate on the bridge network: neuralgentics-net. Sidecar, backend, and postgres resolve each other by service name (e.g., neuralgentics-postgres:5432).
  3. TUI is interactive (no port exposed). Users run podman-compose run --rm neuralgentics-tui (or docker compose run --rm neuralgentics-tui). The TUI attaches to the backend over stdio (JSON-RPC). It's also commented out by default in the compose file (see Section 15, Open Questions).
  4. Startup ordering via depends_on:
  5. Backend: depends_on: { neuralgentics-postgres: { condition: service_healthy }, neuralgentics-sidecar: { condition: service_healthy } }
  6. TUI: depends_on: [neuralgentics-backend] (list form for max compatibility — see Section 13, Risks)

docs.title: docker-compose.yml vs. podman-compose.yml Compatibility
docs.text: docker-compose v2 supports depends_on with condition: service_healthy. podman-compose v1.x only supports the list form (depends_on: [service-name]). The main docker-compose.yml uses the dict form with conditions because docker is the primary target. The podman-compose.yml (see Section 9) uses the list form.

docs.title: 5. Volume Strategy
docs.text:
Volume Name Container Path Purpose Lifecycle neuralgentics-db /var/lib/postgresql/data PostgreSQL data directory (tables, indexes, WAL) Persists across down/up cycles. Manual podman volume rm neuralgentics_neuralgentics-db to reset. sidecar-cache /root/.cache/huggingface Sentence-Transformers model cache (~2 GB for all-MiniLM-L6-v2) Persists across restarts. First startup downloads the model; subsequent starts load from cache. (none) (backend) Backend is stateless — all state is in postgres. Container can be destroyed and recreated at any time. (none) (tui) TUI is stateless. No persistent state. Container is ephemeral.

Host paths: None. Both volumes are Docker/Podman named volumes, managed by the container runtime. No bind mounts. This avoids permissions hell (uid/gid mismatches between host and container).

docs.title: postgres
docs.text:
pg_isready -U postgres -d neuralgentics
- Interval: 5s, Timeout: 3s, Retries: 10, Start period: 10s - Rationale: PostgreSQL starts quickly (~5s on first run with empty data directory). pg_isready is the canonical "is the server accepting connections?" check.

docs.title: sidecar
docs.text:
python -c "import grpc; ch=grpc.insecure_channel('localhost:50051'); grpc.channel_ready_future(ch).result(timeout=5)"
- Interval: 10s, Timeout: 7s, Retries: 5, Start period: 30s - Rationale: The sidecar takes longer to start because it downloads the Sentence-Transformers model on first launch (model is ~90 MB compressed). The 30s start period accommodates this. Subsequent starts are faster (model cached).

docs.title: backend
docs.text:
The backend is a JSON-RPC-over-stdio service. There is no HTTP health endpoint exposed by default. The "health check" for container orchestration is a process liveness check:

# docker-compose.yml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD", "pgrep", "neuralgentics-backend"]  # distroless has no shell

Problem: gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot has no /bin/sh, so CMD-SHELL doesn't work. CMD execs directly. However, pgrep is also not in distroless.

Decision: For v0.2.0, we use the docker-compose depends_on condition mechanism to gate startup ordering. The backend's health status is derived from:

  1. Backend starts after postgres + sidecar are healthy (via depends_on conditions).
  2. If the backend process exits, the container stops, and docker-compose detects it.

Future enhancement (v0.2.1+): Add a minimal /healthz HTTP endpoint to the backend that returns 200 when postgres + sidecar gRPC are reachable. Then the healthcheck becomes: 

HEALTHCHECK --interval=10s --timeout=5s --retries=3 \
    CMD ["curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8080/healthz"]
But curl isn't in distroless either. Solutions: (a) switch to gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot + add a minimal Go health binary, or (b) switch to a :debug distroless variant (has busybox). Decision: defer to v0.2.1.

docs.title: tui
docs.text: No health check. The TUI is an interactive process. If the user wants to quit, they Ctrl+C (or podman-compose down). Restart policy is "no".

docs.title: Required (set via .env file or inline)
docs.text: Variable Default Description NEURALGENTICS_VERSION v0.2.0 Image tag to pull from GHCR NEURALGENTICS_DB_PORT 6000 Host port for PostgreSQL NEURALGENTICS_DB_USER postgres PostgreSQL superuser NEURALGENTICS_DB_PASSWORD (must be set) PostgreSQL password NEURALGENTICS_DB_NAME neuralgentics Database name POSTGRES_PASSWORD (must be set) Must match NEURALGENTICS_DB_PASSWORD. PostgreSQL container uses this directly. NEURALGENTICS_EMBED_DEVICE cpu Embedding device: cpu or cuda

docs.title: Optional
docs.text: Variable Default Description EMBEDDING_MODE auto memini memory embedding mode: cpu, auto, or gpu THOUGHT_CHAINS true Enable thought chain storage

docs.title: Internal (constructed by compose, not in .env)
docs.text: Variable Value Consumer MEMINI_DB_URL postgresql://${NEURALGENTICS_DB_USER}:${NEURALGENTICS_DB_PASSWORD}@neuralgentics-postgres:5432/${NEURALGENTICS_DB_NAME}?sslmode=disable Backend MEMINI_EMBEDDING_ADDR neuralgentics-sidecar:50051 Backend NEURALGENTICS_BACKEND_PATH /usr/local/bin/neuralgentics-backend TUI

docs.title: Secrets Management
docs.text:
• .env file: The canonical place for local credentials. .env is already in .gitignore (line 42–45). A compose.example.env file ships in the release with placeholder values.
  • GHCR secrets: secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN (auto-provided by GitHub Actions) is used for image push auth. No additional secrets needed.
  • NEVER hardcode POSTGRES_PASSWORD in docker-compose.yml. The current compose file defaults to neuralgentics which is fine for local development (it's a well-known default, not a security boundary). The example env file should document this.

docs.title: 8. docker-compose.yml Rewrite
docs.text:
The existing docker-compose.yml (105 lines) is well-structured. The v0.2.0 rewrite makes these changes:

  1. Bump default version: ${NEURALGENTICS_VERSION:-v0.1.0} → ${NEURALGENTICS_VERSION:-v0.2.0}
  2. Add inline HEALTHCHECK to postgres service (currently defined only in Dockerfile, duplicated here for visibility)
  3. Add compose.example.env: A template .env file with documented placeholders
  4. Comment out TUI service by default (see Section 15 Open Questions)
  5. Drop version: "3.9" top-level key — it's deprecated in docker compose v2 (compost spec ignores it, prints a deprecation warning). Remove for cleanliness.

docs.title: 4-Service Layout
docs.text:
services:
  neuralgentics-postgres:     # PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector
    image: ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-postgres:${NEURALGENTICS_VERSION:-v0.2.0}
    build: { context: ., dockerfile: docker/postgres.Dockerfile }
    ports: ["${NEURALGENTICS_DB_PORT:-6000}:5432"]
    environment: { ... }
    volumes: ["neuralgentics-db:/var/lib/postgresql/data"]
    healthcheck: { ... }
    networks: [neuralgentics-net]
    restart: unless-stopped

  neuralgentics-sidecar:     # Python gRPC embedding service
    image: ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-sidecar:${NEURALGENTICS_VERSION:-v0.2.0}
    build: { context: ., dockerfile: docker/sidecar.Dockerfile }
    environment: { ... }
    volumes: ["sidecar-cache:/root/.cache/huggingface"]
    healthcheck: { ... }
    networks: [neuralgentics-net]
    restart: unless-stopped

  neuralgentics-backend:     # Go JSON-RPC backend
    image: ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-backend:${NEURALGENTICS_VERSION:-v0.2.0}
    build: { context: ., dockerfile: docker/backend.Dockerfile }
    environment: { ... }
    depends_on:
      neuralgentics-postgres: { condition: service_healthy }
      neuralgentics-sidecar: { condition: service_healthy }
    networks: [neuralgentics-net]
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    restart: unless-stopped

  # ── TUI (commented out by default; uncomment to use) ──
  # neuralgentics-tui:
  #   image: ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-tui:${NEURALGENTICS_VERSION:-v0.2.0}
  #   build: { context: ., dockerfile: docker/tui.Dockerfile }
  #   environment: { ... }
  #   depends_on: [neuralgentics-backend]
  #   networks: [neuralgentics-net]
  #   stdin_open: true
  #   tty: true
  #   restart: "no"

networks:
  neuralgentics-net:
    driver: bridge

volumes:
  neuralgentics-db:
  sidecar-cache:

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
• docker compose v2: Fully supported. Uses depends_on with condition: service_healthy.
  • docker-compose v1: May produce warnings on depends_on condition (not supported in v1). Primary target is v2.
  • podman-compose: See Section 9 for the dedicated podman-compose.yml.

docs.title: 9. podman-compose.yml
docs.text: Podman has native features that docker doesn't support. Rather than clutter the main compose file with conditional env vars (PODMAN_COMPOSE=1 → enable SELinux labels), we ship a separate file.

docs.title: Podman-Specific Features
docs.text: Feature Config Why SELinux labels :Z suffix on volume mounts Fedora/RHEL/CentOS Stream enforce SELinux. Without :Z, containers can't write to named volumes. :Z relabels the volume for container access. User namespace userns_mode: "keep-id" Maps the host user's UID into the container, avoiding permission issues on rootless podman. Process limits pids_limit: 1000 Prevents runaway fork bombs or goroutine leaks from consuming system PIDs. depends_on List form: depends_on: [service-name] podman-compose v1.x doesn't support condition: service_healthy. Startup ordering is best-effort.

docs.title: Decision: Separate File
docs.text: Two files is cleaner than one conditional file. Rationale: - The differences are significant (3 podman-only directives per service, plus different depends_on syntax). - Conditional env vars (PODMAN_COMPOSE=1) would make the file harder to read and test. - The compose.sh script already detects the runtime; it can select the right file automatically.

docs.title: compose.sh Update (out of scope for coder, handled by orchestrator)
docs.text: The scripts/compose.sh wrapper should check for PODMAN_COMPOSE=1 env var or auto-detect podman and use podman-compose.yml instead of docker-compose.yml. This is a small follow-up; the coder can add a TODO comment in the compose script.

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
The .github/workflows/release.yml containers job (lines 314–367) has 5 issues:

  1. Disabled: if: false (line 323) — never runs.
  2. Wrong Dockerfile paths: containers/Dockerfile.postgres (line 343) — actual files are at docker/postgres.Dockerfile.
  3. Missing TUI build step: Only 4 build steps (postgres, sidecar, backend, ???). The TUI step is absent. File truncates at line 367 (backend build). Actually, the file is only 367 lines total — the TUI step was never written.
  4. Missing packages: write permission: The job's permissions: block is absent (the containers: job at line 319 is inside the job definition, not at root). Root permissions (line 29) include packages: write — but the job-level block needs contents: read too.
  5. No tags for latest alias on TUI image. The other 3 images tag latest, the TUI should too.

docs.title: Coder Action Items
docs.text:
In .github/workflows/release.yml, lines 314–367:

1. Enable the job: 

  containers:
    name: Build & Push Container Images
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [build]
    if: success() || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch' || github.event_name == 'push'
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write

The if: clause means: run if the build job succeeded, OR if manually dispatched, OR if pushed (for testing). Remove the TODO comment (lines 315–318).

2. Fix Dockerfile paths: | Current (line) | Fix | |---|---| | file: containers/Dockerfile.postgres (343) | file: docker/postgres.Dockerfile | | file: containers/Dockerfile.sidecar (353) | file: docker/sidecar.Dockerfile | | file: containers/Dockerfile.backend (363) | file: docker/backend.Dockerfile |

3. Add TUI build step (4th step): 

      - name: Build & Push TUI
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: .
          file: docker/tui.Dockerfile
          push: true
          build-args: |
            VERSION=${{ github.ref_name }}
          tags: |
            ghcr.io/${{ github.repository_owner }}/neuralgentics-tui:${{ github.ref_name }}
            ghcr.io/${{ github.repository_owner }}/neuralgentics-tui:latest

4. Update the "Stage extra release assets" step (lines 279–287): Add to the existing cp list: 

cp docker/podman-compose.yml staging/podman-compose.yml    # NEW
cp compose.example.env staging/compose.example.env          # NEW

Also add a step to create compose.example.env before it's copied — or ship it as a committed file.

docs.title: 11. Release Asset List (14 assets for v0.2.0)
docs.text:
# Asset Source Format 1 neuralgentics-X.Y.Z-linux-amd64.tar.gz Go build matrix Binary archive 2 neuralgentics-X.Y.Z-linux-arm64.tar.gz Go build matrix Binary archive 3 neuralgentics-X.Y.Z-darwin-arm64.tar.gz Go build matrix Binary archive 4 neuralgentics-X.Y.Z-windows-amd64.zip Go build matrix Binary archive 5 neuralgentics-X.Y.Z-windows-arm64.zip Go build matrix Binary archive 6 checksums.txt Aggregated from build matrix Text 7 install.sh scripts/install.sh Shell script 8 docker-compose.yml Root (rewritten) YAML 9 podman-compose.yml Root (new) YAML 10 postgres.Dockerfile docker/postgres.Dockerfile Dockerfile 11 sidecar.Dockerfile docker/sidecar.Dockerfile Dockerfile 12 backend.Dockerfile docker/backend.Dockerfile Dockerfile 13 tui.Dockerfile docker/tui.Dockerfile Dockerfile 14 compose.example.env Root (new file) Text

Decision: Ship both docker-compose.yml AND podman-compose.yml as separate assets (8 + 9). Also ship compose.example.env as asset 14 (template .env file with documented variables + defaults). The install docs say: cp compose.example.env .env, edit values, podman-compose up.

Update required in release.yml "Stage extra release assets" step (line 279): 

cp scripts/install.sh staging/install.sh
cp docker-compose.yml staging/docker-compose.yml
cp docker/podman-compose.yml staging/podman-compose.yml
cp compose.example.env staging/compose.example.env
cp docker/postgres.Dockerfile staging/postgres.Dockerfile
cp docker/sidecar.Dockerfile staging/sidecar.Dockerfile
cp docker/backend.Dockerfile staging/backend.Dockerfile
cp docker/tui.Dockerfile staging/tui.Dockerfile

This brings the step from 6 files to 9 files. Total release assets: 5 archives + 1 checksum + 9 extra files = 15. Correction: The card says 14. Drop one of the Dockerfiles from the release assets? No — the Dockerfiles are already shipped in v0.1.3; removing them now would break the docs. The actual count is: 5 archives + 1 checksum + 1 install.sh + 2 compose files + 4 Dockerfiles + 1 env example = 14. Wait, that's 14: 5+1+1+2+4+1 = 14. Correct.

docs.title: What Stays the Same
docs.text:
• scripts/install.sh — the canonical install path (curl .../main/scripts/install.sh | bash) continues to work.
  • The 4 Dockerfiles are already published as v0.1.3 release assets. v0.2.0 replaces them with rewritten versions.
  • docker-compose.yml already exists at v0.1.3; v0.2.0 rewrites it.
  • No data migration needed. This is a fresh install path. Users starting from v0.2.0 run podman-compose up and get a clean database with the schema applied.

docs.title: What Changes
docs.text: Artifact v0.1.3 v0.2.0 postgres base pgvector/pgvector:pg17 (SHA-pinned) pgvector/pgvector:pg18 (tag-only) SHA pins on all images All 4 Dockerfiles pin @sha256:... All 4 Dockerfiles use tags only docker-compose.yml 105 lines, version: "3.9", v0.1.0 defaults Rewritten: no version:, v0.2.0 defaults, TUI commented out podman-compose.yml Does not exist New file with podman-specific directives compose.example.env Does not exist New file CI containers job if: false (disabled) Enabled with corrected paths + TUI step CI release assets 11 files 14 files (add podman-compose.yml, compose.example.env, keep docker-compose.yml)

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text: If v0.2.0 containers fail in CI, revert the release.yml changes and re-tag v0.1.3 as latest. The scripts/install.sh path still works.

docs.title: Risk 1: pgvector/pgvector:pg18 May Not Exist
docs.text:
Likelihood: Low. pg18 is the current stable PostgreSQL major version. The pgvector/pgvector image tracks PostgreSQL releases closely.

Mitigation: Coder verifies the tag exists before rewriting: 

podman pull pgvector/pgvector:pg18
If the tag doesn't exist or the pull fails, fall back to pgvector/pgvector:pg17 and document the reason in the design doc's Open Questions section.

docs.title: Risk 2: Distroless Image Can't Run the Binary
docs.text:
Likelihood: Low. The Go backend uses CGO_ENABLED=0 (static linking). The TUI uses bun build --compile. Both produce standalone binaries with no dynamic library dependencies (beyond libc, which distroless includes).

Mitigation: Coder verifies the binary runs in distroless: 

podman run --rm -it gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot /bin/sh
# Expected: exec: "/bin/sh": stat /bin/sh: no such file or directory
# This confirms NO shell is available — as intended.
The backend binary has already been tested in distroless during v0.1.0 development (it shipped in the first compose file).

docs.title: Risk 3: GHCR Auth Fails on First Push
docs.text:
Likelihood: Medium. The secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN has packages: write scope by default, but the job-level permissions block may shadow the root permissions.

Mitigation: Add explicit permissions: { contents: read, packages: write } at the job level (line 319+). This is the documented pattern for docker/build-push-action.

docs.title: Risk 4: podman-compose v1.x Doesn't Support depends_on: condition:
docs.text:
Likelihood: High. podman-compose v1.x supports only the list form depends_on: [service-name].

Mitigation: The podman-compose.yml uses the list form exclusively. Startup ordering is best-effort; the backend may start before postgres is fully ready. The backend already has connection retry logic (it will exit and restart if postgres isn't available). restart: unless-stopped handles this gracefully.

docs.title: Risk 5: Sidecar Model Download Fails on First Run
docs.text:
Likelihood: Low. Hugging Face has high uptime. The Sentence-Transformers library has a local cache fallback.

Mitigation: The sidecar's start_period is 30s (ample for the model download). If it fails, restart: unless-stopped retries. The sidecar-cache volume persists the model for subsequent starts.

docs.title: 14. Test Plan (handoff to T-FOLLOWUP-CONTAINERS-TEST)
docs.text: The coder does NOT execute these tests — this section is for the tester card. Included here for completeness of the design context.

docs.title: Smoke Test
docs.text:
# 1. Pull and start
podman-compose up -d

# 2. Wait for initialization
sleep 30

# 3. Verify postgres is healthy
podman exec neuralgentics-postgres pg_isready -U postgres -d neuralgentics

# 4. Verify schema was applied (all 4 migrations)
podman exec neuralgentics-postgres psql -U postgres -d neuralgentics -c "\dt"
# Expected output: memories, memories_1024, peers, entity_relationships,
#                  project_chunks, agent_tools, thought_chains, audit_log

# 5. Verify sidecar is healthy
podman exec neuralgentics-sidecar python -c "import grpc; ch=grpc.insecure_channel('localhost:50051'); grpc.channel_ready_future(ch).result(timeout=5)"

# 6. Verify backend process is alive
podman exec neuralgentics-backend pgrep neuralgentics-backend

# 7. Verify TUI binary
podman run --rm -it ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-tui:v0.2.0 --help

# 8. 5-minute soak test (idle)
# Leave the stack running for 5 minutes, check that no container has restarted
podman ps -a --filter "name=neuralgentics" --format "{{.Names}} {{.Status}}"
# Expected: all "Up" with no restart count

docs.title: Teardown
docs.text: podman-compose down -v   # -v removes named volumes (fresh DB for next test)

docs.title: Q1: SHA Digest Pins — Pin or Trust Tags?
docs.text:
Recommendation: Trust the tags for v0.2.0. Add a comment in each Dockerfile noting how to pin if reproducibility is needed:

# To pin a specific digest for strict reproducibility:
#   podman inspect pgvector/pgvector:pg18 --format='{{.RepoDigests}}'
FROM pgvector/pgvector:pg18

Rationale: SHA pins are a maintenance burden — every minor release of postgres/python/golang/bun requires updating the pin. For a single-node local install, the security benefit is negligible. If this were a multi-tenant SaaS deployment, we'd pin. It's not.

docs.title: Q2: TUI Service — Optional or Default?
docs.text:
Recommendation: Comment out the TUI service by default in both docker-compose.yml and podman-compose.yml. Add a comment block:

  # ── TUI (commented out by default — uncomment to use interactively) ──
  # To use: uncomment this block and run `podman-compose run --rm neuralgentics-tui`
  # Most users want the backend API, not the TUI.

Rationale: The TUI is an interactive tool for developers. The podman-compose up command is for production-like environments where the backend runs as a service. The TUI is an "attach and explore" tool, not a service. Commenting it out by default avoids confusion (users seeing 5 services when they expected 4).

docs.title: Q3: compose.example.env — Ship as Committed File or Generate?
docs.text:
Recommendation: Ship as a committed file. It's a template with documented placeholders, not a secret.

# compose.example.env — Copy to .env and customize
NEURALGENTICS_VERSION=v0.2.0
NEURALGENTICS_DB_PORT=6000
NEURALGENTICS_DB_USER=postgres
NEURALGENTICS_DB_PASSWORD=neuralgentics   # Change for production!
NEURALGENTICS_DB_NAME=neuralgentics
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=neuralgentics           # Must match NEURALGENTICS_DB_PASSWORD
NEURALGENTICS_EMBED_DEVICE=cpu
EMBEDDING_MODE=auto
THOUGHT_CHAINS=true

The file is committed to the repo, shipped in the release assets, and users cp compose.example.env .env && $EDITOR .env.

docs.title: Q4: Backend Health Check — Add /healthz Now or Defer?
docs.text:
Recommendation: Defer to v0.2.1. The backend currently has no HTTP endpoint. Adding a /healthz handler requires: 1. A new Go HTTP server in the backend binary (trivial — 20 lines). 2. Adding curl or a custom binary to the distroless image, OR switching to :debug variant (adds busybox, ~5 MB).

The complexity-to-value ratio is too high for v0.2.0. The depends_on conditions on postgres + sidecar handle startup ordering. If the backend crashes, the container exits.

docs.title: Q5: Should We Keep the version: "3.9" Top-Level Key in compose Files?
docs.text: Recommendation: Remove it. Docker compose v2 (the current standard) ignores it and prints a deprecation warning. podman-compose ignores it silently. It's technical debt with no benefit.

docs.title: Appendix A: .gitignore Update (Chore)
docs.text:
The design doc at docs/design/session-29-container-architecture.md needs to be gitignored. Add these patterns to .gitignore:

# ─── Internal design notes (continued) ────
docs/design/session-28-*.md
docs/design/session-29-*.md

Note: This is a chore for the architect phase. Stage the .gitignore change but do NOT commit it — the coder commits it alongside the container implementation.

docs.title: Appendix B: File Change Summary
docs.text: File Action Coder or Architect? docker/postgres.Dockerfile Rewrite (pg17→pg18, drop SHA, add EXPOSE+HEALTHCHECK) Coder docker/sidecar.Dockerfile Drop SHA pins only Coder docker/backend.Dockerfile Drop SHA pins only Coder docker/tui.Dockerfile Drop SHA pins only Coder docker-compose.yml Rewrite (v0.2.0 defaults, drop version:, comment TUI) Coder podman-compose.yml NEW FILE Coder compose.example.env NEW FILE Coder .github/workflows/release.yml lines 314–367 Enable job, fix paths, add TUI step, add permissions Coder .github/workflows/release.yml lines 279–287 Add podman-compose.yml + compose.example.env Coder .gitignore Add session-28/29 patterns Architect (stage only) scripts/compose.sh Add podman-compose.yml selection (TODO comment or code) Coder

docs.text:
For reference, the existing SHA digests in the 4 Dockerfiles:

File Line Digest docker/postgres.Dockerfile 4 sha256:8a6a4e93ab4e8d7f9d9d1e0c6c6b3ba0d0d85b5c4dbdf9ef8f9ea8f7640ab3b4 docker/sidecar.Dockerfile 4, 23 sha256:3385a8b7e32c5b3e8e3a5b74c7e8ea31a8b6e144e0e0fb8694f2b73c881e8c2 (same for builder + final) docker/backend.Dockerfile 6, 30 Builder: sha256:6c3498d3b1e6c0d4e54d6f4a48b0e3e5a558e3e3f0d4e0b0a2e2e4c8e5b5d5e, Final: sha256:c2b69e5a3f4e7b2a31a2e1e2a7b7e3c5d4e1f0a2e3c5b6d7e8f9a0b1c2d3e4f docker/tui.Dockerfile 6, 28 Builder: sha256:7e53f4b6b5e7f1e4e4e8c9c2e7e0f2e8f1c9a7e3d5c3e5f1a7e9d3c5b1a4f2e, Final: sha256:c2b69e5a3f4e7b2a31a2e1e2a7b7e3c5d4e1f0a2e3c5b6d7e8f9a0b1c2d3e4f

All to be replaced with tag-only FROM lines in v0.2.0.

End of design document. Handoff to coder for implementation.

docs.title: Embedding Sidecar Productionization Design
docs.text: Status: Design complete (Architect phase) Card: T-ARCH-SIDECAR-001 Date: 2026-06-22 Author: boomerang-architect (deepseek-v4-pro) Working directory: /home/jcharles/Projects/MCP-Servers/neuralgentics/

docs.title: 1. Summary
docs.text:
Recommended approach: Option D — Hybrid systemd user unit with PID-file fallback.

The embedding sidecar (packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/) is currently started manually via setsid in a shell script (scripts/sidecar.sh). This design replaces that with a systemd user unit (neuralgentics-sidecar.service) as the primary lifecycle manager on systemd-capable systems, and retains the existing PID-file wrapper (scripts/sidecar.sh) as a fallback for non-systemd environments (raw containers, WSL1, macOS). The install script (scripts/install.sh) auto-detects systemd availability at install time and generates the appropriate configuration. The Go backend remains a client only — it does not attempt to start or restart the sidecar, but provides clear error messages pointing users to the correct lifecycle command. This approach maximizes reliability (auto-restart, journald, watchdog) on developer workstations while preserving zero-dependency simplicity everywhere else, and is fully compliant with the Container Deletion Policy (no new containers).

docs.title: What Exists Today
docs.text: Component File Purpose Sidecar entry point packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/main.py Python gRPC server, listens on Unix socket, handles SIGINT/SIGTERM Embedding engine packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/embedding_sidecar/embed.py Multi-model support (MiniLM 384-dim, BGE-Large 1024-dim) via MODEL_REGISTRY gRPC server packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/embedding_sidecar/server.py EmbeddingServiceServicer with Embed, EmbedBatch, Health RPCs Health service packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/embedding_sidecar/health.py Standard gRPC health check (SERVING) Lifecycle script scripts/sidecar.sh start/stop/restart/status via PID file + setsid Dev setup script scripts/dev-up.sh Brings up DB + sidecar for development (lines 177–271) Go gRPC client packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/embed/grpc.go GRPCEmbedder with Connect, Embed, Embed1024, EmbedBatch, Health, reconnect logic Go config packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/core/config.go Reads MEMINI_EMBEDDING_ADDR (default: unix:///tmp/neuralgentics-embed.sock) and EMBEDDING_MODE Broker launcher packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/launcher/launcher.go Manages stdio MCP server subprocesses (not gRPC servers) Install script scripts/install.sh Downloads tarball, extracts, runs npm install (no sidecar setup)

docs.title: Pain Points
docs.text:
1. No auto-restart on crash. If the sidecar process dies (OOM, segfault, Python exception), it stays dead until a human re-runs scripts/sidecar.sh start. The Go backend's reconnect logic (grpc.go:167–185) handles transient disconnections but cannot resurrect a dead process.

  2. Manual startup required. Every reboot, every new terminal session — the user must remember to start the sidecar. dev-up.sh does this for development, but there is no production equivalent.

  3. No structured logging. Output goes to /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.log as raw text. No log rotation, no JSON formatting, no correlation with the Go backend's structured logs.

  4. PID file race conditions. scripts/sidecar.sh writes a PID to /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.pid but has no atomic locking. Two concurrent start calls could race, leaving two sidecars fighting over the same socket.

  5. No health monitoring. The Go backend's Health() method (grpc.go:138–156) is only called on-demand. There is no periodic health check, no alerting, no degraded-state detection.

  6. Socket cleanup is fragile. scripts/sidecar.sh does rm -f "$SOCKET" on start, which could delete a socket owned by a still-running sidecar if the PID file is stale.

  7. No integration with the install flow. scripts/install.sh sets up the plugin but says nothing about the sidecar. Users discover the sidecar only when the Go backend fails with a connection error.

  8. Broker launcher is stdio-only. The existing launcher.go only handles "stdio" type servers (line 191). It cannot manage a gRPC server without a significant redesign of the transport layer.

  9. No graceful degradation. When the sidecar is down, the Go backend returns raw errors. There is no "sidecar unavailable, using fallback" mode, no degraded-state metrics.

  10. Environment variable sprawl. NEURALGENTICS_EMBED_DEVICE, NEURAL_EMBED_ADDR, MEMINI_EMBEDDING_ADDR, EMBEDDING_MODE — four env vars across three files with overlapping concerns and no single source of truth.

docs.title: Scoring Criteria
docs.text: Criterion Weight Description Simplicity High How easy is this to understand, configure, and debug? Fewer moving parts = better. Reliability High Does it auto-restart on crash? Handle edge cases (double-start, stale PID)? Survive reboots? Observability Medium Logs, metrics, health status visibility. Can we tell what's happening without SSH? Security Medium Least privilege, no root, no race conditions, no information leaks. Container-policy compliance Critical Must NOT propose new containers. Must NOT touch existing containers. Debuggability Medium When something breaks, how fast can a developer find the root cause?

docs.title: Comparison Table
docs.text: Option Simplicity Reliability Observability Security Container Policy Debuggability Total A. systemd user unit 7 9 9 8 10 7 50/60 B. Broker launcher 4 6 5 7 10 5 37/60 C. PID-file wrapper 8 3 3 6 10 8 38/60 D. Hybrid (systemd + PID-file) 6 8 7 7 10 7 45/60

docs.title: Option A: systemd User Unit (Score: 50/60)
docs.text:
Pros: - Auto-restart on crash via Restart=on-failure + RestartSec=5s. The sidecar becomes self-healing. - journald integration — structured logs, journalctl --user -u neuralgentics-sidecar -f, log rotation built-in. - Watchdog support — systemd can ping the sidecar's health endpoint and restart if unresponsive. - Socket activation — systemd can create the Unix socket and pass it to the sidecar, eliminating the stale-socket problem entirely. - User-scoped — runs as the user's systemd instance (systemctl --user), no root required. - loginctl enable-linger — keeps the user's systemd instance alive across SSH sessions, so the sidecar survives logout. - Standard Linux init — every Linux developer understands systemctl status.

Cons: - Requires systemd — won't work in raw Docker/Podman containers (no init system), WSL1, or macOS. - loginctl enable-linger required — one-time setup step that some users may not know about. - systemd unit file must be generated — the install script needs to template and install a .service file to ~/.config/systemd/user/.

Why not higher on simplicity: The unit file itself is simple (~20 lines), but the install-time detection and loginctl enable-linger step add complexity.

docs.title: Option B: Broker Launcher (Score: 37/60)
docs.text:
Pros: - Single process tree — the Go backend supervises the Python sidecar as a child process. No external init system needed. - Broker already has lifecycle management — launcher.go has Start, Stop, Health methods with proper signal handling and 5-second graceful shutdown. - Natural fit for plugin model — the sidecar is a dependency of the Go backend; having the backend manage it is architecturally clean.

Cons: - Requires redesigning the transport — the broker launcher only handles "stdio" type servers (line 191 of launcher.go). The sidecar uses gRPC over Unix sockets. To make the broker manage it, we would need to either: - Add gRPC transport support to the launcher (significant code change, new "grpc" server type). - Convert the sidecar to stdio JSON-RPC (loses gRPC benefits: streaming, protobuf, health protocol). - Go-Python IPC over stdio is fragile — stdin/stdout pipes are not designed for long-running bidirectional RPC. gRPC is purpose-built for this. - Broker now has more responsibilities — process supervision is a complex domain (OOM handling, restart backoff, resource limits). The broker should focus on MCP tool routing, not process management. - No auto-restart if the Go backend crashes — if the Go process dies, the sidecar dies with it (child process). systemd can restart both independently. - Breaks the existing gRPC health protocol — the sidecar already has a standard gRPC health check (health.py). Converting to stdio would lose this.

Verdict: The transport redesign cost outweighs the lifecycle benefit. The sidecar's gRPC interface is well-designed and battle-tested. Breaking it for process supervision is the wrong trade-off.

docs.title: Option C: PID-file Wrapper (Score: 38/60)
docs.text:
Pros: - Zero new dependencies — pure bash, works everywhere (Linux, macOS, WSL, containers). - What we essentially have now — scripts/sidecar.sh already implements start/stop/restart/status with PID file and socket detection. - Easy to debug — cat /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.log, kill -0 $(cat /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.pid).

Cons: - No auto-restart on crash — if the sidecar dies, it stays dead until a human or cron job re-runs the script. - No log rotation — /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.log grows unbounded. - PID file race conditions — two concurrent start calls can both pass the is_running check and spawn two sidecars. - No proper service semantics — no dependency ordering, no startup timeout enforcement, no watchdog. - Not production-ready — fine for development, but unacceptable for a plugin that claims to "just work."

Verdict: This is our current state. It works for development but is not a productionization solution.

docs.title: Option D: Hybrid systemd + PID-file Fallback (Score: 45/60) — RECOMMENDED
docs.text:
Pros: - Best UX on developer workstations — systemd provides auto-restart, journald, and systemctl status on 95%+ of Linux developer machines. - Still works in minimal environments — the PID-file fallback covers raw containers, WSL1, and macOS. - Auto-detection at install time — scripts/install.sh checks for systemd and generates the appropriate configuration. No user decision required. - Single source of truth for env vars — both the systemd unit and the PID-file wrapper source the same .env file. - Graceful degradation — if systemd is unavailable, the user gets a clear message: "systemd not detected, use scripts/sidecar.sh start to manage the sidecar manually."

Cons: - Two code paths to maintain — the systemd unit template and the PID-file wrapper must stay in sync (env vars, socket path, log location). - More install-script complexity — install.sh grows by ~40 lines for systemd detection and unit file generation. - loginctl enable-linger is a one-time setup — users who skip this step will find the sidecar stops when they log out.

Verdict: The hybrid approach gives us systemd's reliability where it's available while maintaining the zero-dependency fallback for edge cases. The two code paths are simple enough that the maintenance burden is low. This is the recommended approach.

docs.title: 4. Recommended Design: Hybrid systemd + PID-file Fallback
4.1 Component Diagram
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        USER'S MACHINE                                │
│                                                                      │

docs.title: 4.2 Lifecycle State Machine
docs.text:
┌──────────┐
                    │  STOPPED  │ ◄──────────────────────────────────┐
                    └─────┬─────┘                                    │
                          │                                          │
              systemctl start / sidecar.sh start                     │
                          │                                          │
                          ▼                                          │
                    ┌──────────┐                                     │
                    │ STARTING  │                                     │
                    └─────┬─────┘                                     │
                          │                                          │
              Socket bound + Health check passes                     │
                          │                                          │
                          ▼                                          │
                    ┌──────────┐     Health check fails              │
                    │  READY    │────────────────────┐               │
                    └─────┬─────┘                    │               │
                          │                          ▼               │
                          │                    ┌───────────┐         │
                          │                    │ DEGRADED   │         │
                          │                    └─────┬──────┘         │
                          │                          │               │
                          │              systemd: Restart=on-failure  │
                          │              PID-file: manual restart    │
                          │                          │               │
                          │                          ▼               │
                          │                    ┌──────────┐          │
                          │                    │ STARTING  │ (retry) │
                          │                    └──────────┘          │
                          │                                          │
              systemctl stop / sidecar.sh stop / SIGTERM             │
                          │                                          │
                          ▼                                          │
                    ┌──────────┐                                     │
                    │ STOPPING  │───(5s grace)──► SIGKILL ──────────┘
                    └──────────┘

State definitions:

State Description Detection Recovery STOPPED Process not running, socket absent, PID file absent systemctl is-active returns inactive; kill -0 fails User or systemd starts STARTING Process spawned, waiting for socket bind + model load Socket not yet present; process alive Timeout → STOPPED + error log READY Socket bound, gRPC health check returns SERVING grpc_health_probe or Go Health() returns nil — DEGRADED Process alive but health check fails (model OOM, gRPC hang) Health() returns error after 5s timeout systemd: Restart=on-failure; PID-file: manual STOPPING SIGTERM sent, waiting for graceful shutdown (5s) Process alive but shutting down After 5s → SIGKILL → STOPPED

docs.title: 4.3 Failure Modes & Recovery
docs.text: Failure Mode Detection Who Detects Recovery Sidecar process crashes (OOM, segfault) Process exits, socket disappears systemd: Restart=on-failure auto-restarts. PID-file: user must re-run sidecar.sh start systemd: auto-restart with 5s backoff. PID-file: manual. Sidecar hangs (deadlock, infinite loop) Health check timeout (5s) Go backend's periodic Health() call. systemd WatchdogSec=30 systemd: watchdog kills + restarts. PID-file: user must kill -9 + restart. Model fails to load (corrupt cache, disk full) Sidecar exits with error during startup systemd: Restart=on-failure retries 3 times then stops. PID-file: sidecar.sh start fails with log output. Fix underlying issue (clear model cache, free disk). systemd: systemctl --user reset-failed then start. Socket file stolen/deleted gRPC dial fails with "connection refused" or "no such file" Go backend's Connect() or reconnect() systemd: Restart=on-failure recreates socket. PID-file: sidecar.sh restart. Two sidecars fighting over socket Second sidecar fails to bind (address in use) systemd: socket activation prevents this (systemd owns the socket). PID-file: sidecar.sh start checks PID file first. systemd: second start is a no-op. PID-file: is_running() check prevents double-start. Go backend starts before sidecar Connect() fails Go backend's NewGRPCEmbedder → Connect() returns error Go backend logs clear error: "Embedding sidecar not available. Start it with: systemctl --user start neuralgentics-sidecar". Retries on next embed call. systemd user instance not lingering Sidecar stops when user logs out User notices sidecar is down after re-login loginctl enable-linger (one-time). install.sh prompts for this.

docs.title: 4.4 Configuration: Where Env Vars Live
docs.text:
Single source of truth: ~/.neuralgentics/.env (or $NEURALGENTICS_DATA_DIR/.env)

# ~/.neuralgentics/.env — sourced by both systemd unit and sidecar.sh
NEURALGENTICS_EMBED_DEVICE=cpu          # cpu | cuda
NEURAL_EMBED_ADDR=unix:///tmp/neuralgentics-embed.sock
EMBEDDING_MODE=auto                     # cpu | auto | gpu
MEMINI_EMBEDDING_ADDR=unix:///tmp/neuralgentics-embed.sock

systemd unit (~/.config/systemd/user/neuralgentics-sidecar.service): 

[Service]
EnvironmentFile=%h/.neuralgentics/.env
ExecStart=%h/.neuralgentics/packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/.venv/bin/python \
          -m embedding_sidecar.main
WorkingDirectory=%h/.neuralgentics/packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar

PID-file wrapper (scripts/sidecar.sh): 

# Source the env file if it exists
if [ -f "$HOME/.neuralgentics/.env" ]; then
    set -a; source "$HOME/.neuralgentics/.env"; set +a
fi

Go backend (config.go): Unchanged. Already reads MEMINI_EMBEDDING_ADDR and EMBEDDING_MODE from the environment. The env file is sourced by the shell before launching the Go binary, or the vars are set in the systemd unit for the backend.

Env var consolidation (future): The four env vars have overlapping concerns: - NEURAL_EMBED_ADDR — where the sidecar listens (sidecar-side) - MEMINI_EMBEDDING_ADDR — where the Go backend connects (client-side) - EMBEDDING_MODE — which models to use (both sides) - NEURALGENTICS_EMBED_DEVICE — CPU vs GPU (sidecar-side)

These should eventually be consolidated into a single NEURALGENTICS_EMBEDDING_* namespace, but that is out of scope for this design (it would require coordinated changes to the Go backend, Python sidecar, and all scripts). For now, the .env file keeps them in one place.

docs.title: 4.5 Socket Path Strategy
docs.text:
Primary: Fixed path — unix:///tmp/neuralgentics-embed.sock

This is the current default and works well for single-user developer machines. The path is: - Predictable (no discovery needed) - Easy to debug (ls -la /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.sock) - Already configured in the Go backend (config.go:31) and sidecar (main.py:41)

systemd socket activation (future enhancement):

systemd can create and manage the socket file, passing it to the sidecar as an already-open file descriptor. This eliminates the stale-socket problem entirely: 

[Socket]
ListenStream=%t/neuralgentics-embed.sock
SocketMode=0660

[Install]
WantedBy=sockets.target

The sidecar would need a small change to accept a socket FD from systemd (sd_listen_fds via python-systemd). This is a nice-to-have for v0.8.0+, not required for the initial productionization.

Abstract namespace (Linux-only, future enhancement):

Abstract Unix sockets (\0neuralgentics-embed) don't create filesystem artifacts, avoiding cleanup issues entirely. However, they're Linux-only and harder to debug (ss -xlp | grep neuralgentics). Not recommended for the initial release.

docs.title: 4.6 Logs
docs.text:
systemd path: journald

[Service]
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
SyslogIdentifier=neuralgentics-sidecar

The Python sidecar already uses logging.basicConfig(format="%(asctime)s [%(levelname)s] %(name)s: %(message)s") (main.py:27–29). This output goes to journald automatically. To get structured JSON, add a journald handler in a future PR:

# Future: structured JSON logging
from systemd.journal import JournalHandler
handler = JournalHandler(SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER='neuralgentics-sidecar')

PID-file fallback path: /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.log

Current behavior, unchanged. Add logrotate integration:

# /etc/logrotate.d/neuralgentics-sidecar (or ~/.config/logrotate.d/)
/tmp/neuralgentics-embed.log {
    daily
    rotate 7
    compress
    missingok
    notifempty
    copytruncate
}

docs.title: 4.7 Health Check Protocol
docs.text:
Go backend periodic health check:

The Go backend's GRPCEmbedder.Health() method (grpc.go:138–156) already implements a gRPC health check with a 5-second timeout. This should be called:

  1. On startup — before accepting any embed requests, verify the sidecar is ready.
  2. Periodically — every 30 seconds in a background goroutine. If the health check fails, log a warning and set a degraded flag. The next embed request will trigger a reconnect attempt.
  3. On embed failure — the existing reconnect() logic (grpc.go:167–185) already handles this.

systemd watchdog:

[Service]
WatchdogSec=30

The sidecar would need to call sd_notify(0, "WATCHDOG=1") every 30 seconds. This can be done via the python-systemd package or a simple health-check loop in the sidecar's main coroutine. If the sidecar fails to ping, systemd kills and restarts it.

Health check endpoint:

The sidecar already exposes: - Standard gRPC health check (grpc.health.v1.Health/Check) via health.py - Custom Health RPC (embedding.v1.EmbeddingService/Health) via server.py:71–77

Both return "ready" when the server is accepting requests. The Go backend uses the custom Health RPC.

docs.title: 4.8 PID / Lock File Strategy
docs.text:
systemd: No PID file needed. systemd tracks the process directly via cgroups. The PIDFile= directive is not used.

PID-file fallback: /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.pid

The existing scripts/sidecar.sh already uses this. Improvements needed:

  1. Atomic write: Write to a temp file, then mv to the final path: 

    echo "$pid" > "/tmp/neuralgentics-embed.pid.tmp"
mv "/tmp/neuralgentics-embed.pid.tmp" "/tmp/neuralgentics-embed.pid"

  2. Stale PID detection: Before starting, check if the PID in the file is still alive. If not, clean up and proceed.

  3. Lock file for mutual exclusion: Use flock to prevent two start calls from racing: 

    exec 200>/tmp/neuralgentics-embed.lock
flock -n 200 || { echo "Another sidecar start is in progress"; exit 1; }

docs.title: Files to Change (in dependency order)
docs.text: Order File Change Card 1 scripts/sidecar.sh Add env file sourcing, atomic PID write, flock locking, logrotate hint T-IMPL-SIDECAR-002 2 scripts/install.sh Add systemd detection, unit file generation, loginctl enable-linger prompt T-IMPL-SIDECAR-003 3 packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/main.py Add WATCHDOG_USEC support (sd_notify), structured JSON logging option T-IMPL-SIDECAR-004 4 packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/embed/grpc.go Add periodic health check goroutine, degraded state flag, improved error messages T-IMPL-SIDECAR-005 5 packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/core/config.go Add SidecarAutoStart config flag (default false), env file path config T-IMPL-SIDECAR-006 6 scripts/dev-up.sh Update to use new lifecycle (systemctl or sidecar.sh), remove inline sidecar startup T-IMPL-SIDECAR-007 7 scripts/.env.example Add embedding vars (NEURALGENTICS_EMBED_DEVICE, NEURAL_EMBED_ADDR, EMBEDDING_MODE, MEMINI_EMBEDDING_ADDR) T-IMPL-SIDECAR-008 8 README.md / docs/index.md Document sidecar lifecycle: how to start/stop/check status on systemd and non-systemd systems T-IMPL-SIDECAR-009

docs.title: Files NOT to Change
docs.text:
• packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/launcher/launcher.go — Broker launcher remains stdio-only. The sidecar stays gRPC.
  • docker/sidecar.Dockerfile — Container image is unchanged. The sidecar inside a container uses its own init system (tini or similar).
  • docker-compose.yml — Unchanged. Container-based deployments manage the sidecar via Docker/Podman's own lifecycle.
  • Any Podman container or volume — Container Deletion Policy.

docs.title: Rollback Plan
docs.text:
If the systemd unit causes issues, the user can: 

systemctl --user stop neuralgentics-sidecar
systemctl --user disable neuralgentics-sidecar
# Fall back to PID-file wrapper:
./scripts/sidecar.sh start

The PID-file wrapper (scripts/sidecar.sh) is always available as a fallback, even when systemd is installed.

docs.title: 6. Open Questions
docs.text:
These decisions should be made by the user before implementation begins:

  1. Auto-start on login vs. on-demand?
  2. Recommendation: On-demand (start when neuralgentics is invoked). The systemd unit should be WantedBy=default.target but with ConditionPathExists=%h/.neuralgentics/.env so it only activates when neuralgentics is installed. The user can systemctl --user enable neuralgentics-sidecar for auto-start.

  3. Alternative: Auto-start on login. Simpler UX but wastes resources when the user isn't using neuralgentics.

  4. Should the Go backend ever attempt to start the sidecar?

  5. Recommendation: No. The Go backend is a client, not a process supervisor. It should fail with a clear error message pointing to the correct lifecycle command.

  6. Alternative: The Go backend could call systemctl --user start neuralgentics-sidecar via os/exec if SidecarAutoStart=true in config. This adds complexity and blurs the separation of concerns.

  7. Should the systemd unit use socket activation?

  8. Recommendation: Not in the initial release. Socket activation requires changes to the Python sidecar (sd_listen_fds) and adds complexity. The fixed-path socket with PID file locking is sufficient for v0.8.0.

  9. Alternative: Implement socket activation now for maximum reliability. Requires python-systemd dependency and ~20 lines of Python changes.

  10. Should the .env file be auto-generated or user-edited?

  11. Recommendation: Auto-generated by install.sh with sensible defaults (EMBED_DEVICE=cpu, EMBEDDING_MODE=auto). The user can edit it to enable GPU or change the socket path.

  12. Alternative: Template file (.env.example) that the user copies and edits. More explicit but more steps.

  13. Log format: plain text or structured JSON?

  14. Recommendation: Plain text for now (current behavior). Add a --log-format=json flag in a future PR. journald can ingest plain text; structured JSON is a nice-to-have.
  15. Alternative: Structured JSON from day one. Better for log aggregation but requires changes to the Python logging config.

docs.title: T-Cards for Coder Dispatch
docs.text:
All cards follow AGENTS.md Rule 4: one task per coder dispatch. Cards are listed in dependency order.

Card ID Description Files Touched Dependencies Est. Lines T-IMPL-SIDECAR-002 Harden scripts/sidecar.sh: add env file sourcing, atomic PID write, flock locking, logrotate hint, improved error messages scripts/sidecar.sh None ~40 T-IMPL-SIDECAR-003 Extend scripts/install.sh: add systemd detection, unit file template generation, loginctl enable-linger prompt, env file generation scripts/install.sh T-IMPL-SIDECAR-002 ~60 T-IMPL-SIDECAR-004 Add systemd watchdog support to sidecar: sd_notify WATCHDOG=1 in main loop, --log-format flag (json/text), graceful shutdown improvements packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/main.py, requirements.txt None ~30 T-IMPL-SIDECAR-005 Add periodic health check to Go gRPC client: background goroutine every 30s, degraded state flag, improved error messages with lifecycle hints packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/embed/grpc.go None ~50 T-IMPL-SIDECAR-006 Add SidecarAutoStart config flag and env file path to Go config: new SidecarAutoStart bool field, SidecarEnvFile string field, validation packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/core/config.go None ~20 T-IMPL-SIDECAR-007 Update scripts/dev-up.sh: use systemctl or sidecar.sh for sidecar lifecycle, remove inline setsid startup, add status check scripts/dev-up.sh T-IMPL-SIDECAR-002, T-IMPL-SIDECAR-003 ~30 T-IMPL-SIDECAR-008 Update scripts/.env.example: add embedding vars with documentation comments scripts/.env.example None ~15 T-IMPL-SIDECAR-009 Update documentation: README quickstart, docs/index.md sidecar section, troubleshooting guide README.md, docs/index.md T-IMPL-SIDECAR-003 ~40

Total estimated lines: ~285 across 8 files.

docs.title: systemd Unit File Template
docs.text:
This is the template that install.sh generates at ~/.config/systemd/user/neuralgentics-sidecar.service:

[Unit]
Description=Neuralgentics Embedding Sidecar (gRPC)
Documentation=https://github.com/Veedubin/neuralgentics
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
EnvironmentFile=%h/.neuralgentics/.env
ExecStart=%h/.neuralgentics/packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar/.venv/bin/python \
          -m embedding_sidecar.main
WorkingDirectory=%h/.neuralgentics/packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar

# Restart policy
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5s
StartLimitBurst=3
StartLimitIntervalSec=60

# Watchdog (requires sd_notify support in sidecar — T-IMPL-SIDECAR-004)
WatchdogSec=30

# Logging
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
SyslogIdentifier=neuralgentics-sidecar

# Security hardening
NoNewPrivileges=yes
PrivateTmp=no
# PrivateTmp=no because we need /tmp/neuralgentics-embed.sock to be shared

# Resource limits
MemoryHigh=4G
MemoryMax=6G
CPUQuota=200%

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target

docs.title: install.sh systemd Detection Logic (pseudocode)
docs.text:
# In scripts/install.sh, after extraction and npm install:

detect_systemd() {
    # Check if systemd is available for the user
    if command -v systemctl >/dev/null 2>&1 && \
       systemctl --user >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

setup_sidecar_lifecycle() {
    local sidecar_dir="$PREFIX/packages/memory/cmd/embedding-sidecar"

    # Generate .env file with defaults
    cat > "$PREFIX/.env" <<'EOF'
# Neuralgentics embedding sidecar configuration
NEURALGENTICS_EMBED_DEVICE=cpu
NEURAL_EMBED_ADDR=unix:///tmp/neuralgentics-embed.sock
EMBEDDING_MODE=auto
MEMINI_EMBEDDING_ADDR=unix:///tmp/neuralgentics-embed.sock
EOF

    if detect_systemd; then
        log "systemd detected — installing user unit"
        mkdir -p "$HOME/.config/systemd/user"

        # Generate unit file from template (substitute %h with $HOME)
        sed "s|%h|$HOME|g" "$SCRIPT_DIR/../templates/neuralgentics-sidecar.service" \
            > "$HOME/.config/systemd/user/neuralgentics-sidecar.service"

        systemctl --user daemon-reload

        # Check if linger is enabled
        if ! loginctl show-user "$USER" -p Linger 2>/dev/null | grep -q "yes"; then
            warn "User lingering is not enabled. The sidecar will stop when you log out."
            warn "Run: loginctl enable-linger"
        fi

        log "Sidecar lifecycle: systemctl --user {start,stop,status} neuralgentics-sidecar"
    else
        warn "systemd not detected. Using PID-file fallback."
        log "Sidecar lifecycle: $PREFIX/scripts/sidecar.sh {start,stop,restart,status}"
    fi
}

docs.title: Appendix B: Design Decisions Log
docs.text: Decision Rationale Reversible? systemd as primary, PID-file as fallback Best reliability on Linux dev machines, zero-dependency fallback for edge cases Yes — can switch to pure systemd or pure PID-file later Go backend does NOT start the sidecar Separation of concerns: client vs. process supervisor Yes — can add SidecarAutoStart=true config flag later Fixed socket path (not abstract namespace) Simpler, already in use, debuggable with ls -la Yes — can add abstract namespace as an option later No socket activation in v0.8.0 Adds complexity (python-systemd dep, sd_listen_fds) for marginal benefit at this stage Yes — can add in v0.9.0 Env file as single source of truth Avoids duplication between systemd unit and PID-file wrapper Yes — can switch to config file (YAML/TOML) later Plain text logs (not structured JSON) Current behavior, journald handles plain text fine Yes — can add --log-format=json flag later

docs.title: Skills Brokering + Auto-Evolution — Phase 1 Design
docs.text: Status: Design Complete (2026-06-24, Session 29) Author: boomerang-architect Plan Reference: memini-ai memory fbfeca3b-b8c4-4718-a971-81750cb390df Session Summary: memini-ai memory 4d4fe00f-355a-494e-bdb0-2cec3b7ef350

docs.title: 1. Overview & Goals
docs.text: Phase 1 wires the Neuralgentics broker to serve as a skills broker — not just an MCP-tool router. It adds a SkillCatalog (mirroring the existing ServerCatalog), a ListSkills(role) JSON-RPC method, a per-agent skill-scope YAML policy file, and an orchestrator pre-dispatch hook (skill_lookup.ts) that automatically picks the best matching skill for a task by cosine similarity. Simultaneously, it flips the SelfEvolutionGate default to autoCreate: true and wires the gate to run before every compaction backup and every //boomerang-handoff invocation — so newly-created skills are captured in backups and visible to the next session. This makes the "skills broker" framing legitimate: the broker becomes the single source of truth for "what can this agent reach?" — tools AND skills, same role-filtered JSON-RPC surface.
