docs.text:
These decisions were locked in the original plan (memini-ai memory fbfeca3b-b8c4-4718-a971-81750cb390df) and must NOT be re-litigated during implementation:

Decision Value Cadence Every compaction AND every //boomerang-handoff, no cooldown Orchestrator picks automatically By embedding similarity, no confirmation Per-agent scoping Hybrid YAML + SKILL.md front-matter auto_create default true Skill reuse memory shape {type: "skill_reuse", skill, saved_tokens, task} Trust bump target Skill's PROVENANCE memory (not the skill itself) Wiring point Option A: in neuralgentics_dispatch_task handler, after handleTask returns saved_tokens formula max(0, 3000 - len(body + name) / 4) Skill body cap in prompt 4000 chars Error handling recordSkillReuse never throws — best-effort, log and continue Wave 2 dispatch Sequential: T-SB-012 first, T-SB-013 second

docs.title: Transport Architecture & Multi-Provider Registry
docs.text: Version: v0.4.0 Design Date: 2026-06-07 Author: boomerang-architect (deepseek-v4-pro) Repo: Veedubin/neuralgentics (GHCR: veedubin/neuralgentics-*) Working directory: /home/jcharles/Projects/MCP-Servers/neuralgentics

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
This document designs the transport abstraction and multi-provider LLM registry for neuralgentics v0.4.0. The two features are closely related: both require the runtime to pick between multiple implementations (which LLM to use, which transport to launch an MCP with). They share the same TUI picker pattern and the same broker extension points.

User mandate (verbatim): "Just get it done. It's mostly related. Maybe we could let the user decide if they want to do NPX/UVX, install locally, or Docker MCPs. We can have all of it. We should add DMR as another provider. I also think we should add in OpenRouter support."

Key constraints: - All 4 Go modules must remain green after every card. - Zero-Error Rule: gates failing → fix inline or block the card. - opencode.json provider ID is ollama-cloud (with hyphen).

docs.title: 1.1 Current State
docs.text:
File: .opencode/opencode.json lines 3-42 

"provider": {
  "ollama-cloud": {
    "npm": "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
    "name": "Ollama Cloud",
    "options": { "baseURL": "https://ollama.com/v1" },
    "models": { ... }
  }
}

File: .opencode/opencode.json line 118: 

"small_model": "ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b-cloud"

The current provider block is a single-key object. There is no runtime selection—the opencode provider ID is hardcoded.

docs.title: 1.2 New Shape
docs.text:
Replace "provider": { "ollama-cloud": {...} } with a plural "providers" object where each key is the provider id and each value follows an isomorphic shape. Add a "defaultProvider": "ollama-cloud" field so OpenCode knows which provider to use as the default.

"defaultProvider": "ollama-cloud",
"providers": {
  "ollama-cloud": {
    "npm": "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
    "name": "Ollama Cloud",
    "url": "https://ollama.com/v1",
    "type": "openai-compatible",
    "description": "Ollama Cloud API (default, primary)",
    "models": {
      "kimi-k2.6:cloud":       { "name": "Kimi K2.6 (Cloud)" },
      "glm-5.1:cloud":         { "name": "GLM 5.1 (Cloud)" },
      "deepseek-v4-pro:cloud": { "name": "DeepSeek V4 Pro (Cloud)" },
      "devstral-2:123b-cloud": { "name": "Devstral 2 (Cloud)" },
      "deepseek-v4-flash:cloud": { "name": "DeepSeek V4 Flash (Cloud)" },
      "qwen3-coder-next:cloud": { "name": "Qwen3 Coder Next (Cloud)" },
      "minimax-m2.7:cloud":    { "name": "MiniMax M2.7 (Cloud)" },
      "gemma4:31b-cloud":      { "name": "Gemma 4 31B (Cloud)" },
      "qwen3.5:cloud":         { "name": "Qwen 3.5 (Cloud)" },
      "devstral-small-2:24b-cloud": { "name": "Devstral Small 2 (Cloud)" }
    }
  },
  "dmr-local": {
    "npm": "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
    "name": "Docker Model Runner (Local)",
    "url": "http://localhost:12434/engines/v1",
    "type": "openai-compatible",
    "description": "Docker Model Runner for local inference via GPU",
    "models": {
      "ai/qwen2.5-coder:7b":       { "name": "Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B (Local)" },
      "ai/llama3.2:3b":            { "name": "Llama 3.2 3B (Local)" },
      "ai/devstral-small-2:24b":   { "name": "Devstral Small 2 24B (Local)" }
    }
  },
  "openrouter": {
    "npm": "@openrouter/ai-sdk-provider",
    "name": "OpenRouter",
    "url": "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1",
    "type": "openrouter",
    "apiKeyEnv": "OPENROUTER_API_KEY",
    "description": "OpenRouter aggregator for multi-model access",
    "models": {
      "anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet":          { "name": "Claude 3.5 Sonnet" },
      "openai/gpt-4o":                        { "name": "GPT-4o" },
      "google/gemini-pro-1.5":                { "name": "Gemini Pro 1.5" },
      "meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b-instruct":   { "name": "Llama 3.1 405B" },
      "mistralai/mistral-large-latest":       { "name": "Mistral Large" }
    }
  }
}

Design decisions: - Provider key names match the OpenCode provider ID format (used in agent .md files as model: ollama-cloud/kimi-k2.6:cloud). - dmr-local uses type: "openai-compatible" because DMR exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint at http://localhost:12434/engines/v1. - openrouter uses type: "openrouter" because the @openrouter/ai-sdk-provider npm package wraps the Vercel AI SDK with OpenRouter-specific auth header injection (API key via Authorization: Bearer). - openrouter has a apiKeyEnv: "OPENROUTER_API_KEY" field that the TUI/plugin reads to pass the API key to the provider. - Model names use the exact API model IDs (e.g. anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet for OpenRouter, ai/qwen2.5-coder:7b for DMR).

docs.title: 1.3 small_model Update
docs.text:
Before (line 118): 

"small_model": "ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b-cloud"

After: 

"small_model": "ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b-cloud"

This stays unchanged for backward compatibility. The TUI /provider command does not change small_model—only the active provider for agent dispatching.

docs.title: 1.4 TUI Runtime Provider Picker
docs.text:
New slash command: /provider [name|list|status]

Sub-commands: | Command | Action | |---------|--------| | /provider | Show current active provider | | /provider list | List all providers with health status | | /provider <name> | Switch active provider to <name> | | /provider status | Show all 3 providers with health checks |

Health checks implemented via the broker: - ollama-cloud: HTTP GET https://ollama.com/v1/models → expect 200 - dmr-local: HTTP GET http://localhost:12434/engines/v1/models → expect 200 (or connection refused = offline) - openrouter: HTTP GET https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models with Authorization: Bearer $OPENROUTER_API_KEY → expect 200

Provider persistence: - The TUI writes the active provider to ~/.config/neuralgentics/provider-pref.json: {"activeProvider": "ollama-cloud"}. - On startup, the TUI reads this file. If absent, defaults to the defaultProvider from opencode.json. - The orchestrator reads the active provider from provider-pref.json and writes the provider ID into the agent's Context Package. - The Context Package includes: provider: "ollama-cloud", model: "ollama-cloud/kimi-k2.6:cloud".

Env var override: NEURALGENTICS_DEFAULT_PROVIDER=openrouter (takes precedence over config file if set; useful for CI).

File reference: Provider picker reuses the pattern from the /model command handler at packages/tui/src/commands.ts lines 766-885 (handleModelCommand / handleModelCommandAsync).

docs.title: 1.5 Agent Model Format Update
docs.text:
Currently, agent .md files use: 

model: ollama/kimi-k2.6:cloud

This does NOT change for v0.4.0. The ollama/ prefix maps to the OpenCode provider ID ollama-cloud. In the future, agents could be tagged for specific providers: 

model: openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet

This is out of scope for v0.4.0—the /provider TUI command controls which provider OpenCode uses for ALL agents.

docs.title: 2.1 Current State
docs.text:
File: .opencode/opencode.json lines 45-85

Each MCP entry is a single flat object with type, command, environment:

"github-mcp": {
  "type": "local",
  "command": ["npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
  "environment": { "GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN": "{env:GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN}" },
  "enabled": true
}

File: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/types/types.go lines 3-12 

type ServerConfig struct {
    Name         string
    Command      string
    Args         []string
    Env          map[string]string
    Type         string   // "stdio", "http", "sse"
    Description  string
    Capabilities []string
}

File: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/launcher/launcher.go lines 160-195

buildCommand() currently only handles Type: "stdio" with exec.Command(config.Command, config.Args...). The "http" and "sse" cases are empty (commented as "future phase").

docs.title: 2.2 New Transport Shape
docs.text:
Replace the flat MCP entry with a transports array:

"github-mcp": {
  "enabled": true,
  "transports": [
    {
      "type": "npx",
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
      "default": true,
      "environment": {
        "GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN": "{env:GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN}"
      }
    },
    {
      "type": "docker",
      "image": "ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-github-mcp:v0.1.0",
      "runtime": "auto",               // "docker" | "podman" | "auto" (= detect)
      "args": ["--github-token", "$GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN"],
      "environment": {}
    },
    {
      "type": "local",
      "bin": "github-mcp-server",
      "installHint": "cargo install github-mcp-server",
      "environment": {}
    },
    {
      "type": "uvx",
      "package": "github-mcp-server",
      "args": [],
      "environment": {}
    }
  ]
}

Transport types defined:

Type Spawn Method Config Fields Description npx npx -y <package> command (optional, default "npx"), args, environment Node.js MCP via npx (existing pattern) uvx uvx <package> command (optional, default "uvx"), args, environment Python MCP via uvx local ./binary bin (path to binary), installHint (string), environment Pre-installed binary (e.g. ~/.local/bin/) docker docker run -i --rm <image> image, runtime (docker/podman/auto), args, environment Containerized MCP http HTTP POST to URL url (string), headers (optional map) Hosted MCP (e.g. Cloudflare Workers) sse SSE stream from URL url (string), headers (optional map) Server-Sent Events MCP

Transport selection: - The first transport with "default": true is the default. If none has this flag, the first in the list is the default. - The user picks at runtime via /mcp activate <name> --transport npx. - If the selected transport fails (binary missing, docker not running, HTTP 503), the broker auto-falls-back to the next transport in the list (ordered by the array position).

Transport merge: When a user activates a specific transport, that transport's config is merged into a single types.ServerConfig for the launcher. The launcher's buildCommand is extended to handle each transport type.

docs.title: 2.3 Go Type Updates
docs.text:
NEW: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/types/types.go (extends existing 28-line file):

// TransportType defines how an MCP server process is launched.
type TransportType string

const (
    TransportNPX    TransportType = "npx"
    TransportUVX    TransportType = "uvx"
    TransportLocal  TransportType = "local"
    TransportDocker TransportType = "docker"
    TransportHTTP   TransportType = "http"
    TransportSSE    TransportType = "sse"
)

// TransportConfig holds a single transport option for launching an MCP server.
type TransportConfig struct {
    Type        TransportType       `json:"type"`
    Command     string              `json:"command,omitempty"`     // npx/uvx command
    Args        []string            `json:"args,omitempty"`
    Env         map[string]string   `json:"environment,omitempty"`
    Bin         string              `json:"bin,omitempty"`         // local binary path
    InstallHint string              `json:"installHint,omitempty"` // how to install the local binary
    Image       string              `json:"image,omitempty"`       // docker image name
    Runtime     string              `json:"runtime,omitempty"`     // "docker" | "podman" | "auto"
    URL         string              `json:"url,omitempty"`         // http/sse URL
    Headers     map[string]string   `json:"headers,omitempty"`     // http/sse headers
    Default     bool                `json:"default,omitempty"`     // default transport for this MCP
    Status      string              `json:"status,omitempty"`      // "available" | "unavailable" | "unknown"
}

// MCPServerConfig wraps the legacy flat config with multi-transport support.
type MCPServerConfig struct {
    Name        string            `json:"name"`
    Enabled     bool              `json:"enabled"`
    Transports  []TransportConfig `json:"transports"`
    // Legacy fields for backward compatibility (auto-converted to TransportConfig).
    LegacyType    string            `json:"type,omitempty"`
    LegacyCommand []string          `json:"command,omitempty"`
    LegacyEnv     map[string]string `json:"environment,omitempty"`
}

Existing ServerConfig is preserved (no deletion—used by broker internally after transport resolution). The new MCPServerConfig wraps it.

docs.title: 2.4 Launcher Extensions
docs.text:
File: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/launcher/launcher.go lines 160-195

Extend buildCommand() with per-transport handling:

func buildCommandForTransport(tc types.TransportConfig) (*exec.Cmd, io.WriteCloser, io.ReadCloser, error) {
    switch tc.Type {
    case types.TransportNPX:
        return buildStdioCommand(tc.Command, tc.Args, tc.Env) // defaults to "npx"

    case types.TransportUVX:
        return buildStdioCommand(tc.Command, tc.Args, tc.Env) // defaults to "uvx"

    case types.TransportLocal:
        cmd := exec.Command(tc.Bin, tc.Args...)
        // ... same pipe setup as stdio ...

    case types.TransportDocker:
        runtime := detectContainerRuntime(tc.Runtime) // "docker" or "podman"
        args := []string{"run", "-i", "--rm"}
        for k, v := range tc.Env {
            args = append(args, "-e", k+"="+v)
        }
        args = append(args, tc.Image)
        args = append(args, tc.Args...)
        cmd := exec.Command(runtime, args...)
        // ... pipe setup ...

    case types.TransportHTTP, types.TransportSSE:
        // HTTP/SSE: no subprocess. Validate URL, mark as "ready" in registry.
        // The proxy layer handles HTTP/SSE calls directly via http.Client.
        // Return a sentinel indicating no subprocess needed.
        return nil, nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("http transport: no subprocess (use proxy layer)")

    default:
        return nil, nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("unknown transport type: %q", tc.Type)
    }
}

func detectContainerRuntime(preferred string) string {
    if preferred == "docker" { return "docker" }
    if preferred == "podman" { return "podman" }
    // Auto-detect: prefer docker, fallback to podman.
    if _, err := exec.LookPath("docker"); err == nil { return "docker" }
    if _, err := exec.LookPath("podman"); err == nil { return "podman" }
    return "docker" // fallback, will fail with "docker: command not found" (correct error).
}

Transport fallback logic: The broker's new ActivateMCPServer() method iterates the transport list, attempts each, and records which succeeded. If all fail, returns a multi-transport error.

docs.title: 2.5 Backward Compatibility
docs.text:
Auto-conversion rules in the broker:

  1. type: "local" with command: ["npx", ...] → auto-converts to transport: { type: "npx", command: "npx", args: ["-y", "<pkg>"], default: true }.
  2. type: "local" with command: ["uvx", ...] → auto-converts to transport: { type: "uvx", command: "uvx", args: ["<pkg>"], default: true }.
  3. type: "local" with command: ["./binary"] → auto-converts to transport: { type: "local", bin: "./binary", default: true }.
  4. All existing environment fields → copied to the auto-generated transport's environment.
  5. enabled flag → preserved as-is at MCP level.

Conversion happens in the broker's config loader, not in the config file. The opencode.json file can still use the old format and the broker will parse it correctly. No existing user config requires change.

Example auto-conversion:

Input: 

"github-mcp": {
  "type": "local",
  "command": ["npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
  "enabled": true
}

Broker internally resolves to: 

MCPServerConfig{
    Name: "github-mcp",
    Enabled: true,
    Transports: []TransportConfig{{
        Type: "npx",
        Command: "npx",
        Args: []string{"-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"},
        Default: true,
    }},
}

docs.title: 3.1 catalog.json — Curated MCP Catalog

New file: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/catalog/catalog.json (committed to repo)

A curated list of 20 popular MCP servers, each with all supported transports:

docs.title: 3.2 Broker Methods
docs.text:
Six new JSON-RPC methods exposed on the broker:

Method Params Returns Description broker.discoverCatalog {role: string} ServerCatalog (filtered by role) List MCPs from catalog.json not yet active broker.activateMCP {name: string, transport: string} {serverName, transport, status} Activate an MCP with the given transport broker.deactivateMCP {name: string} {serverName, wasRunning} Stop MCP, remove from active set broker.listTransports {name: string} [{type, status, default}] List transports for a specific MCP with availability status broker.listActiveMCPs {role: string} [{name, transport, status, tools}] List all currently active MCPs broker.exportProfile {} {profileId, path, size} Export active set to neuralgentics-profile-{timestamp}.tar.gz

docs.title: 3.3 Activate Flow
docs.text:
1. User types /catalog add github-mcp --transport npx
   └─ TUI calls broker.activateMCP({name: "github-mcp", transport: "npx"})

2. Broker reads catalog.json entry for "github-mcp"
   └─ Finds transport with type="npx"

3. Broker checks permissions:
   └─ access.CanAccess(role, "github-mcp")
   └─ If unauthorized: return ErrUnauthorized with available servers hint

4. Broker converts TransportConfig → ServerConfig
   └─ Calls launcher.StartWithTransport(config, transport)

5. Launcher spawns the process, MCP handshake, discovers tools

6. Broker stores entry in registry with active transport metadata

7. Returns {serverName: "github-mcp", transport: "npx", status: "running", tools: 22}

docs.title: 3.4 Deactivate Flow
docs.text:
1. User types /catalog remove github-mcp
   └─ TUI calls broker.deactivateMCP({name: "github-mcp"})

2. Broker checks: is the MCP in the registry?
   └─ Yes → stop process via launcher.Stop(), deregister from registry
   └─ No → return {wasRunning: false}

3. Returns {serverName: "github-mcp", wasRunning: true}

docs.title: 3.5 Profile Export/Import (OCI-shareable)
docs.text:
Export: 

1. Broker serializes active MCP set: [{name, transport, env, startedAt}]
2. Writes to neuralgentics-profile-{timestamp}.tar.gz containing:
   - profile.json (active MCP list)
   - permission-snapshot.json (current role→server mappings)
   - catalog.lock.json (which catalogue entries are active)
3. Returns {profileId, path, size}

Import: 

1. User unpacks .tar.gz
2. Broker reads profile.json
3. For each MCP: calls activateMCP with the recorded transport
4. Permission snapshot is applied via access.Grant() calls
5. Returns confirmation with count of activated MCPs

This is a stretch feature included in the design for completeness. Implementation priority: P2 (after the core activate/deactivate flow).

docs.title: 4.1 Current State
docs.text:
File: packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/access/access.go lines 43-69

DefaultServerRoles maps 7 server names to explicit role lists plus 2 allow-all entries (memoryManager, neuralgentics). The CanAccess method (line 103) checks if a role is in the server's allow list. Orchestrator always has access (line 105).

23 role constants defined at lines 17-41.

docs.title: 4.2 New Permissions
docs.text:
New role: RoleMCPCurator — can mcp.activate from catalog but cannot mcp.deactivate. Use case: a curator can add new tools but not remove existing ones (separation of concerns for shared environments).

const RoleMCPCurator Role = "mcp-curator"

Permission matrix rules:

Role mcp.activate (from catalog) mcp.activate (non-catalog) mcp.deactivate orchestrator ✓ ✓ ✓ developer ✓ (catalog only) ✗ ✓ (own activations only) mcp-curator ✓ ✗ ✗ boomerang-git ✗ ✗ ✗

Implementation: The broker's ActivateMCPServer() checks: 1. If the MCP name is in catalog.json entries → allow for all roles with catalog access. 2. If the MCP name is NOT in catalog.json → only orchestrator can activate (ad-hoc activation). 3. If activating would conflict with an existing restricted server → check CanAccess(role, name).

Granular permission: mcp.activate.<name> — future per-MCP activate permission. Not implemented in v0.4.0 (the catalog-based gate is sufficient). Reserved for v0.5.0.

docs.title: 4.3 Access Control Updates
docs.text:
Add to AllRoles() (access.go line 213): 

RoleMCPCurator,

New DefaultServerRoles entry (no changes needed—catalog-based activation doesn't modify the server role map; it only gates which users can call activateMCP).

New method in AccessControl: 

// CanActivateFromCatalog checks if a role can activate MCPs from the catalog.
func (ac *AccessControl) CanActivateFromCatalog(role string) bool {
    if role == string(RoleOrchestrator) || role == string(RoleMCPCurator) {
        return true
    }
    // developer can activate from catalog (not arbitrary MCPs)
    if role == "developer" {
        return true
    }
    return false
}

// CanDeactivate checks if a role can deactivate an MCP.
func (ac *AccessControl) CanDeactivate(role string, serverName string) bool {
    if role == string(RoleOrchestrator) {
        return true
    }
    // mcp-curator cannot deactivate.
    if role == string(RoleMCPCurator) {
        return false
    }
    // developer can deactivate MCPs they (or the orchestrator) activated.
    // In v0.4.0 we track who activated each MCP; if unknown, allow.
    return true
}

docs.title: 5.1 OpenCode Config
docs.text: Old config format Auto-resolution "provider": { "ollama-cloud": {...} } Broker reads old key, wraps in "providers" with "defaultProvider": "ollama-cloud". OpenCode itself needs "providers" (plural) to support multiple providers; the broker can translate but OpenCode must also be updated to read the new key. "mcp": { "github-mcp": { "type": "local", "command": ["npx", ...] } } Broker auto-converts to single-transport list (see Section 2.5). "small_model": "ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b-cloud" No change.

docs.title: 5.2 Broker Types
docs.text: Old type Behavior types.ServerConfig Preserved unchanged. Used internally after transport resolution. types.ServerConfig.Type: "stdio" Treated as legacy. Launcher auto-detects from command (npx/uvx/binary). launcher.buildCommand() Extended with new transport types; old "stdio" path unchanged.

docs.title: 5.3 TUI Commands
docs.text:
Old command Behavior /model <name> Works as before; model names include provider prefix (unchanged). /memory <query> Unchanged. All 20 existing commands Unchanged.

No existing user config requires change. If a user has a v0.3.1 opencode.json, the broker handles auto-conversion on startup and writes a migration note to stderr (not to the config file).

docs.title: 5.4 Migration to New Format
docs.text:
The new opencode.json format with "providers" (plural) and "transports" arrays is the canonical format. The broker auto-converts old configs but does NOT rewrite the file. Users can manually migrate when convenient.

Migration command (future TUI feature): 

/migrate config
Prompts user, shows diff, writes new opencode.json with .old backup.

docs.title: 6. File-Overlap Analysis & Parallel Dispatch Wave Plan
6.1 Card Definitions
docs.text: Card ID Name Files Touched Lines T-OPENROUTER-PROVIDER Add OpenRouter to provider registry .opencode/opencode.json (new providers block) ~20 new lines in provider section T-DMR-PROVIDER Add DMR-local to provider registry .opencode/opencode.json (same providers block) ~15 new lines in provider section T-COMPOSE-MODELS Add DMR & provider compose services docker-compose.yml ~30 new lines T-TRANSPORT-ABSTRACTION Implement transport types + launcher + backend handlers packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/types/types.go (+40 lines), packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/launcher/launcher.go (+60 lines), packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/broker.go (+120 lines for activate/deactivate), packages/backend-go/cmd/backend/main.go (+80 lines for handler registrations + handlers), packages/tui/src/neuralgentics-client/types.ts (+30 lines for MethodRegistry entries) T-CATALOG-001 Create catalog.json + broker catalog methods + TUI catalog commands packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/catalog/catalog.json (NEW, ~400 lines), packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/broker.go (+50 lines for discover/export), packages/backend-go/cmd/backend/main.go (+60 lines), packages/tui/src/commands.ts (+150 lines for /catalog, /mcp commands) T-DUAL-PROVIDER TUI /provider command + provider picker logic packages/tui/src/commands.ts (+120 lines), packages/tui/src/neuralgentics-client/types.ts (+20 lines) T-DOCS-V040 Update architecture docs, README, CHANGELOG docs/architecture/overview.md, README.md, CHANGELOG.md T-REL-V040 Release bump: version 0.3.1 → 0.4.0, tag, publish package.json (root), packages/tui/package.json, packages/opencode/package.json, 4× Go var version (ldflags in release workflow)

docs.title: 6.2 Overlap Matrix
docs.text:
opencode.json   docker-compose.yml  broker/types  broker/launcher  broker/broker  backend/main  TUI/types  TUI/commands  catalog.json
T-OPENROUTER            ●
T-DMR-PROVIDER          ● (SAME FILE)
T-COMPOSE-MODELS                          ●
T-TRANSPORT-ABSTRACT                                 ●              ●               ●              ●            ●
T-CATALOG-001                                                                       ● (SHARED)      ●           ● (SHARED)    ● (NEW)
T-DUAL-PROVIDER                                                                                                 ●            ● (SHARED)

Overlaps identified: 1. T-OPENROUTER ↔ T-DMR-PROVIDER: Both touch opencode.json lines 3-42 (provider block). NOT parallel-safe. 2. T-TRANSPORT-ABSTRACTION ↔ T-CATALOG-001: Both touch packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/broker.go. NOT parallel-safe. 3. T-TRANSPORT-ABSTRACTION ↔ T-CATALOG-001: Both touch packages/backend-go/cmd/backend/main.go. NOT parallel-safe. 4. T-CATALOG-001 ↔ T-DUAL-PROVIDER: Both touch packages/tui/src/commands.ts. NOT parallel-safe.

docs.title: 6.3 Parallel-Safe Wave Structure
docs.text:
╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║ Wave 1 (PARALLEL — file-disjoint)                             ║
║   T-OPENROUTER-PROVIDER  → boomerang-coder  (opencode.json)   ║
║   T-COMPOSE-MODELS       → boomerang-coder  (docker-compose)  ║
╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ Wave 2 (SEQUENTIAL — waits for T-OPENROUTER)                  ║
║   T-DMR-PROVIDER         → boomerang-coder  (opencode.json)   ║
╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ Wave 3 (SOLO — no file overlap with prior waves)              ║
║   T-TRANSPORT-ABSTRACTION → boomerang-coder                   ║
╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ Wave 4 (SOLO — waits for T-TRANSPORT, shares broker.go)       ║
║   T-CATALOG-001           → boomerang-coder                   ║
╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ Wave 5 (SOLO — waits for T-CATALOG, shares commands.ts)       ║
║   T-DUAL-PROVIDER         → boomerang-coder (commands.ts)     ║
╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ Wave 6 (PARALLEL — file-disjoint from all prior)              ║
║   T-DOCS-V040             → boomerang-writer                  ║
║   T-TEST-ALL              → boomerang-tester (runs all tests) ║
╠════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║ Wave 7 (SOLO — release)                                       ║
║   T-REL-V040              → boomerang-release                 ║
╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Total: 7 waves, 8 cards. Max parallelism: 2 concurrent coders (Wave 1 only).

docs.title: 6.4 Why Only 2-Way Parallelism?
docs.text:
The file-overlap analysis reveals that only Wave 1 has genuinely file-disjoint cards. Every other wave touches at least one shared file with a prior wave. This is significantly fewer parallel waves than the "all parallel" claim from Session 31.

Root cause: The provider block in opencode.json is a single JSON object (lines 3-42) — OpenRouter and DMR share the same key path. The broker's broker.go (427 lines) is the central orchestrator file — transport activation and catalog discovery both extend its methods. The TUI's commands.ts (1625 lines) is the centralized command registry — both catalog and provider commands add to the same file.

Recommendation: Future refactoring that splits broker.go into broker.go (core) + transports.go (transport abstraction) + catalog_commands.go (catalog discovery) would enable more parallelism. This is a v0.5.0 consideration.

docs.title: 7.1 Single Minor Bump
docs.text:
v0.4.0 ships ALL cards together. User explicitly rejected multi-session stretch: "I don't see why we need to stretch it to multiple sessions. Just get it done."

Bump targets: | File | From | To | |------|------|----| | package.json (root) | 0.3.1 | 0.4.0 | | packages/tui/package.json | 0.3.1 | 0.4.0 | | packages/opencode/package.json | 0.3.1 | 0.4.0 | | packages/memory/src/neuralgentics/memory/core/version.go (or var version = "dev") | dev | Set via -ldflags in release workflow | | packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/version.go | dev | Set via -ldflags | | packages/orchestrator-go/src/neuralgentics/orchestrator/version.go | dev | Set via -ldflags | | packages/backend-go/cmd/backend/main.go line 34 | var version = "dev" | Set via -ldflags="-X main.version=v0.4.0" |

Go version auto-inheritance: The release workflow (.github/workflows/release.yml) passes VERSION as a build arg, setting -ldflags="-X main.version=$VERSION". The Go modules with var version = "dev" do NOT change in source — the release pipeline injects the version at build time. ✅ Confirmed: no Go source changes needed for version bumps.

docs.title: 7.2 Artifacts
docs.text:
• Single changelog section for v0.4.0 in CHANGELOG.md
  • Single hero copy update in docs/index.md
  • Single git tag v0.4.0
  • Single release workflow run (GitHub Actions)
  • Container images: ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-backend:v0.4.0, ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-postgres:v0.4.0, ghcr.io/veedubin/neuralgentics-sidecar:v0.4.0

docs.title: 7.3 Plan B: Quality Gate Failure
docs.text:
If quality gates block (tests fail, lint fails, Go build fails):

Scenario Action Config-only cards (T-OPENROUTER, T-DMR, T-COMPOSE) pass, but code cards (T-TRANSPORT, T-CATALOG, T-DUAL-PROVIDER) fail Ship cards 1-3 as v0.3.2 patch (config-only, no code changes). Then cards 4-6 as v0.4.0 minor in a follow-up session. All 6 code cards pass Ship as v0.4.0 minor (single batch). Any card blocks with unfixable error Block the card, ship the rest. The blocked card becomes a follow-up card in the next session.

Plan B is for emergency only. The ideal outcome is all 6 cards passing quality gates and shipping as a single v0.4.0.

(broker-go) tsc --noEmit (TUI) go test ./... (broker-go) go build ./... (broker-go + backend-go) T-CATALOG-001 gofmt -d, go vet ./... (broker-go), eslint (TUI) tsc --noEmit go test ./... (broker-go), bun test (TUI) go build ./... (all 4 modules) + bun test (TUI, 744 tests) go build ./... (all 4 modules)

docs.title: 8.3 Orchestrator Instructions
docs.text:
1. Before dispatching any cards: Run git status to verify clean working tree (or handle dirty state from design work).
  2. Wave 1 dispatch: Two parallel boomerang-coder tasks for T-OPENROUTER-PROVIDER and T-COMPOSE-MODELS. No file overlap. Wait for both to complete before Wave 2.
  3. Between waves: Run gofmt -d, go vet, bun test on changed files to catch regressions early.
  4. Card wrap-up format: Each coder returns {memory_id, description, files_changed, quality_gates: {lint: pass|fail, test: pass|fail, build: pass|fail}}.
  5. After Wave 7: Run npm version minor --no-git-tag-version in root, packages/tui, packages/opencode. Update CHANGELOG.md. Git tag v0.4.0. Push. Trigger release workflow.
  6. Save to memini-ai: After each card completes, save the wrap-up with tags transport-architecture, v0.4.0, and the card ID.

docs.title: 8.4 Risks & Open Questions
docs.text: Risk Mitigation OpenCode providers support: OpenCode's config schema may not support the plural providers key. If OpenCode rejects providers, use the existing provider key with a single default provider and add additionalProviders as a separate key (not read by OpenCode, only by neuralgentics broker). Research before Wave 1. OpenRouter npm package: @openrouter/ai-sdk-provider may not be compatible with @ai-sdk/openai-compatible. Test with npm install @openrouter/ai-sdk-provider before writing the config. If incompatible, fall back to @ai-sdk/openai-compatible with custom headers: { Authorization: "Bearer $OPENROUTER_API_KEY" }. DMR GPU access: Docker containers need GPU passthrough (--gpus all). Add runtime: nvidia option to docker transport for GPU-accelerated MCPs. Note in design that DMR-local itself runs outside Docker (it's a local Docker Model Runner instance). 20 curated MCPs: Some may not have all 4 transports. Each catalog entry only lists transports that actually exist for that MCP. Some may have only 1 transport. Health check reliability: HTTP health checks may fail transiently. Add retry (3 attempts, 1s apart) with exponential backoff. Cache health status for 30s. small_model provider awareness: If user switches to OpenRouter, small_model should pick from OpenRouter's models. Defer to v0.5.0. v0.4.0 keeps small_model hardcoded to ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b-cloud.

docs.title: 8.5 Pre-Wave 0: Research Card (Architect)
docs.text:
Before dispatching Wave 1, the orchestrator MUST dispatch a research-only architect card to verify:

  1. OpenCode supports the providers (plural) config key. If not, design the additionalProviders escape hatch.
  2. @openrouter/ai-sdk-provider npm package exists and is compatible with OpenCode's provider model.
  3. DMR's OpenAI-compatible endpoint at http://localhost:12434/engines/v1 is the correct URL.

Research card ID: T-RESEARCH-V040 (architect, read-only, no code).

End of design document. ~850 lines.

docs.title: v0.5.0 Design: OCI-shareable Profile, HTTP/SSE Transport, small_model Provider Awareness, CI -short Fix
docs.text: Status: Design Phase (Architect Complete) Author: boomerang-architect (deepseek-v4-pro) Date: 2026-06-07 Prerequisite: v0.4.0 shipped (commit 8059ea1, tagged, release workflow in progress)

docs.title: Current State Analysis
docs.text:
Finding: ci.yml line 73 already includes the -short flag:

// File: .github/workflows/ci.yml, line 68-75
- name: Test all Go modules
  run: |
    MODULES="packages/memory packages/orchestrator-go packages/broker-go packages/backend-go"
    for m in $MODULES; do
      echo "::group::Testing $m"
      (cd "$m" && GOWORK=off go test -short -timeout 60s ./...)
      echo "::endgroup::"
    done

The -short flag was apparently added in a previous commit (v0.4.0 or earlier). The release.yml has no test step at all — it only builds binaries and pushes containers.

docs.title: Root Cause (if CI still times out)
docs.text: The -short flag works by having tests check testing.Short() and skip themselves. The pre-existing TestBroker integration test in packages/broker-go/src/neuralgentics/broker/broker_integration_test.go (~13 test files in the broker module) spawns subprocesses and uses proxy.readLoop which blocks on bufio.Scanner. If that test does not call t.Skip() when testing.Short() is true, then -short has no effect and the test will still run in CI.

docs.title: Fix (Two Options)
docs.text:
Option A (Preferred — No Test Changes): - Verify that broker_integration_test.go checks testing.Short(). If it does, no further changes needed — CI is already fixed. - If it does not, add if testing.Short() { t.Skip("skipping integration test in short mode") } to TestBroker.

Option B (If -short doesn't apply): - Split the broker test step in CI into two steps: - Unit tests: go test -short -timeout 60s ./... - Integration tests: go test -run TestBroker -timeout 120s ./... (separate step, longer timeout)

docs.title: File Changes

docs.text:
Constant map (added to commands.ts, near KNOWN_PROVIDERS at line 1930):

const SMALL_MODEL_BY_PROVIDER: Record<string, string> = {
  'ollama-cloud': 'ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b-cloud',
  'dmr-local': 'dmr-local/ai/devstral-small-2:24b',

};

New ProviderPref type (added to types.ts):

export interface ProviderPref {
  activeProvider: string;
  smallModel: string;
  smallModelProvider: string;
  updatedAt: string;
}

Modified writeActiveProvider() (replaces lines 1969-1980):

function writeActiveProvider(name: string): void {
  const prefPath = getProviderPrefPath();
  const smallModel = SMALL_MODEL_BY_PROVIDER[name] ?? SMALL_MODEL_BY_PROVIDER[DEFAULT_PROVIDER];
  mkdirSync(join(prefPath, ".."), { recursive: true });
  writeFileSync(
    prefPath,
    JSON.stringify(
      {
        activeProvider: name,
        smallModel,
        smallModelProvider: name,
        updatedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
      },
      null,
      2,
    ),
  );
}

Modified readActiveProvider() (replaces lines 1950-1963):

function readProviderPref(): ProviderPref {
  const prefPath = getProviderPrefPath();
  if (existsSync(prefPath)) {
    try {
      const pref = JSON.parse(readFileSync(prefPath, "utf-8"));
      // Backward compatibility: v0.4.0 shape didn't have smallModel
      if (typeof pref.activeProvider === "string" && pref.activeProvider) {
        return {
          activeProvider: pref.activeProvider,
          smallModel: pref.smallModel ?? SMALL_MODEL_BY_PROVIDER[pref.activeProvider] ?? SMALL_MODEL_BY_PROVIDER[DEFAULT_PROVIDER],
          smallModelProvider: pref.smallModelProvider ?? pref.activeProvider,
          updatedAt: pref.updatedAt ?? new Date().toISOString(),
        };
      }
    } catch {
      // Fall through to default
    }
  }
  return {
    activeProvider: DEFAULT_PROVIDER,
    smallModel: SMALL_MODEL_BY_PROVIDER[DEFAULT_PROVIDER],
    smallModelProvider: DEFAULT_PROVIDER,
    updatedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
  };
}

docs.title: Trade-off: opencode.json vs provider-pref.json
docs.text:
• opencode.json (small_model key at line 163) is the source of truth for OpenCode's model dispatch. The TUI does NOT modify this file — OpenCode reads it at startup.
  • provider-pref.json is the TUI's runtime source of truth for provider selection and small model mapping.
  • Rationale: opencode.json is a static config file checked into the repo. provider-pref.json is a per-machine runtime preference. The TUI should not mutate checked-in config.
  • Future: When Neuralgentics adds an /opencode write command that regenerates opencode.json from TUI state, the small_model field will be populated from provider-pref.json.smallModel. Deferred to v0.5.1.

Note: No MethodRegistry change required. provider-pref.json is a local file read/write, not a JSON-RPC call.

docs.title: 3.1 HTTP Client (proxy/http_client.go — NEW FILE)
docs.text:
package proxy

import (
    "bufio"
    "bytes"
    "context"
    "encoding/json"
    "fmt"
    "net/http"
    "strings"
    "time"

    "neuralgentics-broker/src/neuralgentics/broker/types"
)

// HTTPClient handles JSON-RPC communication with remote MCP servers over HTTP/SSE.
type HTTPClient struct {
    baseURL    string
    httpClient *http.Client
    sessionID  string
    headers    map[string]string // Authorization, custom headers from Env
}

// NewHTTPClient creates an HTTP client for a remote MCP server.
func NewHTTPClient(baseURL string, headers map[string]string) *HTTPClient {
    return &HTTPClient{
        baseURL: baseURL,
        httpClient: &http.Client{Timeout: 30 * time.Second},
        headers:   headers,
    }
}

// Initialize performs the MCP initialize handshake over HTTP POST.
func (h *HTTPClient) Initialize(ctx context.Context) error {
    params := map[string]any{
        "protocolVersion": "2024-11-05",
        "capabilities":    map[string]any{},
        "clientInfo": map[string]any{
            "name":    "neuralgentics-broker",
            "version": "0.5.0",
        },
    }
    resp, err := h.sendRPC(ctx, "initialize", params)
    if err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("http initialize handshake: %w", err)
    }
    if resp.Error != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("http initialize error: code=%d message=%s", resp.Error.Code, resp.Error.Message)
    }
    // Send initialized notification (fire-and-forget).
    _, _ = h.sendRPC(ctx, "notifications/initialized", nil)
    return nil
}

// ListTools requests the tool list from the remote server.
func (h *HTTPClient) ListTools(ctx context.Context) ([]types.ToolSummary, error) {
    resp, err := h.sendRPC(ctx, "tools/list", map[string]any{})
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("http list tools: %w", err)
    }
    if resp.Error != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("http list tools error: code=%d message=%s", resp.Error.Code, resp.Error.Message)
    }
    var result struct {
        Tools []struct {
            Name        string `json:"name"`
            Description string `json:"description"`
        } `json:"tools"`
    }
    if err := json.Unmarshal(resp.Result, &result); err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("unmarshal tools: %w", err)
    }
    summaries := make([]types.ToolSummary, 0, len(result.Tools))
    for _, t := range result.Tools {
        summaries = append(summaries, types.ToolSummary{
            Server:      h.baseURL,
            Name:        t.Name,
            Description: t.Description,
        })
    }
    return summaries, nil
}

// Call sends a JSON-RPC method call and returns the result.
func (h *HTTPClient) Call(ctx context.Context, method string, params map[string]any) (map[string]any, error) {
    resp, err := h.sendRPC(ctx, method, params)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("http call %q: %w", method, err)
    }
    if resp.Error != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("http error: code=%d message=%s", resp.Error.Code, resp.Error.Message)
    }
    var result map[string]any
    if err := json.Unmarshal(resp.Result, &result); err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("unmarshal result: %w", err)
    }
    return result, nil
}

// CallSSE opens an SSE connection and reads event-stream lines.
// Uses the standard SSE wire format: "data: <json>\n\n".
func (h *HTTPClient) CallSSE(ctx context.Context, method string, params map[string]any) (map[string]any, error) {
    reqBody, err := json.Marshal(jsonrpcRequest{
        JSONRPC: "2.0",
        ID:      1,
        Method:  method,
        Params:  params,
    })
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("marshal SSE request: %w", err)
    }

    httpReq, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodPost, h.baseURL, bytes.NewReader(reqBody))
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("create SSE request: %w", err)
    }
    httpReq.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
    httpReq.Header.Set("Accept", "text/event-stream")
    for k, v := range h.headers {
        httpReq.Header.Set(k, v)
    }

    resp, err := h.httpClient.Do(httpReq)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("SSE request: %w", err)
    }
    defer resp.Body.Close()

    if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("SSE request returned %d", resp.StatusCode)
    }

    // Parse SSE event stream.
    scanner := bufio.NewScanner(resp.Body)
    scanner.Buffer(make([]byte, 0, 64*1024), 1024*1024)
    var lastData []byte
    for scanner.Scan() {
        line := scanner.Text()
        if strings.HasPrefix(line, "data: ") {
            lastData = []byte(strings.TrimPrefix(line, "data: "))
        }
        if line == "" && len(lastData) > 0 {
            // End of event — parse the accumulated data.
            break
        }
    }
    if err := scanner.Err(); err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("SSE scan error: %w", err)
    }
    if len(lastData) == 0 {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("no SSE data received")
    }

    var rpcResp jsonrpcResponse
    if err := json.Unmarshal(lastData, &rpcResp); err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("unmarshal SSE response: %w", err)
    }
    if rpcResp.Error != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("SSE error: code=%d message=%s", rpcResp.Error.Code, rpcResp.Error.Message)
    }
    var result map[string]any
    if err := json.Unmarshal(rpcResp.Result, &result); err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("unmarshal SSE result: %w", err)
    }
    return result, nil
}

// sendRPC marshals, sends, and parses a JSON-RPC request/response over HTTP POST.
func (h *HTTPClient) sendRPC(ctx context.Context, method string, params map[string]any) (*jsonrpcResponse, error) {
    req := jsonrpcRequest{
        JSONRPC: "2.0",
        ID:      1,
        Method:  method,
        Params:  params,
    }
    body, err := json.Marshal(req)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("marshal request: %w", err)
    }

    httpReq, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodPost, h.baseURL, bytes.NewReader(body))
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("create request: %w", err)
    }
    httpReq.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
    for k, v := range h.headers {
        httpReq.Header.Set(k, v)
    }

    httpResp, err := h.httpClient.Do(httpReq)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("http request: %w", err)
    }
    defer httpResp.Body.Close()

    if httpResp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("http %d", httpResp.StatusCode)
    }

    var rpcResp jsonrpcResponse
    if err := json.NewDecoder(httpResp.Body).Decode(&rpcResp); err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("decode response: %w", err)
    }
    return &rpcResp, nil
}

docs.title: 3.2 Client Interface & Dispatch (proxy/proxy.go — ADD TO EXISTING FILE)
docs.text:
Add a Client interface and GetClient factory after the existing MCPProxy type (after line 345):

// Client is the common interface for both stdio and HTTP MCP communication.
type Client interface {
    Initialize(serverName string) error
    ListTools(serverName string) ([]types.ToolSummary, error)
    Call(serverName, method string, params map[string]any) (map[string]any, error)
}

// GetClient returns a Client appropriate for the given ServerConfig's transport type.
func GetClient(config types.ServerConfig) (Client, error) {
    switch config.Type {
    case "http", "sse":
        url := config.Env["NEURALGENTICS_MCP_URL"]
        if url == "" {
            return nil, fmt.Errorf("http transport requires NEURALGENTICS_MCP_URL in env")
        }
        headers := make(map[string]string)
        if auth, ok := config.Env["Authorization"]; ok {
            headers["Authorization"] = auth
        }
        return &httpClientAdapter{
            client: NewHTTPClient(url, headers),
        }, nil
    case "stdio":
        return &stdioClientAdapter{
            proxy: NewMCPProxy(),
        }, nil // Note: the adapter's Initialize will call proxy.Initialize
    default:
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("unknown transport type: %q", config.Type)
    }
}

Adapter types (lives in proxy.go to avoid circular imports):

// httpClientAdapter wraps HTTPClient to implement the Client interface.
type httpClientAdapter struct {
    client *HTTPClient
}

func (a *httpClientAdapter) Initialize(_ string) error {
    return a.client.Initialize(context.Background())
}

func (a *httpClientAdapter) ListTools(serverName string) ([]types.ToolSummary, error) {
    summaries, err := a.client.ListTools(context.Background())
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    // Override server name in summaries.
    for i := range summaries {
        summaries[i].Server = serverName
    }
    return summaries, nil
}

func (a *httpClientAdapter) Call(_, method string, params map[string]any) (map[string]any, error) {
    return a.client.Call(context.Background(), method, params)
}

docs.title: 3.3 Broker Integration
docs.text:
The broker's StartServer method (broker.go lines 85-128) currently assumes stdio: - It calls b.launcher.Start(), then b.proxy.StartReader(), then b.proxy.Initialize().

For HTTP servers, the flow changes: 1. launcher.Start() → calls buildCommand() which returns nil, nil, nil for http/sse 2. The registry entry will have no Process, no Stdin, no Stdout 3. The proxy layer cannot use MCPProxy for stdio operations

New flow for HTTP (added to broker.go as StartHTTPServer or guarded inside StartServer):

func (b *Broker) StartServer(name string) error {
    entry, ok := b.registry.Get(name)
    if !ok {
        return fmt.Errorf("server %q not registered", name)
    }

    // If this is an HTTP/SSE server, use the HTTP client path.
    if entry.Config.Type == "http" || entry.Config.Type == "sse" {
        return b.startHTTPServer(name, entry)
    }

    // Existing stdio path...
    // (unchanged lines 92-128)
}

func (b *Broker) startHTTPServer(name string, entry *registry.ServerEntry) error {
    url := entry.Config.Env["NEURALGENTICS_MCP_URL"]
    if url == "" {
        return fmt.Errorf("server %q has no NEURALGENTICS_MCP_URL", name)
    }
    headers := make(map[string]string)
    if auth, ok := entry.Config.Env["Authorization"]; ok {
        headers["Authorization"] = auth
    }
    httpClient := proxy.NewHTTPClient(url, headers)

    // Initialize handshake.
    if err := httpClient.Initialize(context.Background()); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("initialize http server %q: %w", name, err)
    }

    // Discover tools.
    tools, err := httpClient.ListTools(context.Background())
    if err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("list tools from http %q: %w", name, err)
    }
    // Fix server names in summaries.
    for i := range tools {
        tools[i].Server = name
    }
    entry.Tools = tools
    b.registry.UpdateEntry(name, entry)
    return nil
}

The Call method (broker.go lines 354-385) also needs a guard — if entry.Config.Type == "http", use HTTPClient.Call instead of MCPProxy.Call.

docs.title: 4.1 File Format
docs.text:
Archive: neuralgentics-profile-{timestamp}.tar.gz

Internal structure: 

profile.json           # Active provider, small model, version
catalog.lock.json      # Active MCPs + chosen transports (one per MCP)
permissions.json       # Snapshot of role→server mappings
opencode.snapshot.json # Copy of opencode.json at export time
provider-pref.json     # Copy of provider-pref.json at export time
manifest.json          # Version, exported_at, exported_by (hostname), checksum
signature.bin          # HMAC-SHA256 of all files concatenated (optional)

manifest.json: 

{
  "version": "0.5.0",
  "profile_version": 1,
  "exported_at": "2026-06-07T18:50:00Z",
  "exported_by": "dev-machine-01",
  "exported_by_user": "jcharles",
  "checksum": "sha256:abc123...",
  "signed": true
}

docs.title: 4.2 Go Implementation (proxy/profile/profile.go — NEW FILE)
docs.text:
package profile

import (
    "archive/tar"
    "bytes"
    "compress/gzip"
    "crypto/hmac"
    "crypto/sha256"
    "encoding/hex"
    "encoding/json"
    "fmt"
    "io"
    "os"
    "time"

    "neuralgentics-broker/src/neuralgentics/broker"
    "neuralgentics-broker/src/neuralgentics/broker/access"
    "neuralgentics-broker/src/neuralgentics/broker/types"
)

// ProfileManifest describes the exported profile.
type ProfileManifest struct {
    Version        string `json:"version"`
    ProfileVersion int    `json:"profile_version"`
    ExportedAt     string `json:"exported_at"`
    ExportedBy     string `json:"exported_by"`
    ExportedByUser string `json:"exported_by_user,omitempty"`
    Checksum       string `json:"checksum"`
    Signed         bool   `json:"signed"`
}

// Profile contains all serialized components of an export.
type Profile struct {
    Manifest    json.RawMessage // ProfileManifest
    Provider    json.RawMessage // Active provider + small model
    Catalog     json.RawMessage // Active MCPs + transports
    Permissions json.RawMessage // Role→server snapshot
    Opencode    json.RawMessage // opencode.json snapshot
    Pref        json.RawMessage // provider-pref.json snapshot
}

// Export writes a tar.gz profile to w.
func Export(w io.Writer, brk *broker.Broker, passphrase string) error {
    hostname, _ := os.Hostname()
    user := os.Getenv("USER")

    // Collect state from broker.
    manifest := ProfileManifest{
        Version:        "0.5.0",
        ProfileVersion: 1,
        ExportedAt:     time.Now().UTC().Format(time.RFC3339),
        ExportedBy:     hostname,
        ExportedByUser: user,
        Signed:         passphrase != "",
    }
    manifestJSON, _ := json.MarshalIndent(manifest, "", "  ")

    // Build catalog lock: list of active servers with their chosen transport.
    servers := brk.ListServers()
    // ... serialize active servers to catalog.lock.json

    // Build permissions snapshot.
    ac := brk.AccessControl()
    permSnapshot := map[string][]string{}
    for _, name := range ac.ServerNames() {
        // Get roles for each server — access.go doesn't expose a per-server role getter.
        // During implementation, either add a method or snapshot the internal map.
        permSnapshot[name] = []string{} // placeholder
    }
    permJSON, _ := json.MarshalIndent(permSnapshot, "", "  ")

    // Read opencode.json from disk (passed in or at a well-known path).
    opencodeJSON, _ := os.ReadFile(".opencode/opencode.json")

    // Build pref JSON (provider-pref.json).
    prefJSON, _ := os.ReadFile(providerPrefPath())

    profile := Profile{
        Manifest:    manifestJSON,
        Provider:    prefJSON, // reuse — it already contains provider+smallModel
        Catalog:     nil,      // placeholder
        Permissions: permJSON,
        Opencode:    opencodeJSON,
        Pref:        prefJSON,
    }

    // Write tar.gz.
    return writeTarGz(w, profile, passphrase)
}

// Sign computes HMAC-SHA256 of concatenated profile data.
func Sign(profileBytes []byte, passphrase string) ([]byte, error) {
    mac := hmac.New(sha256.New, []byte(passphrase))
    mac.Write(profileBytes)
    return mac.Sum(nil), nil
}

// Verify checks an HMAC-SHA256 signature against the profile data.
func Verify(profileBytes, signature []byte, passphrase string) bool {
    expected, err := Sign(profileBytes, passphrase)
    if err != nil {
        return false
    }
    return hmac.Equal(expected, signature)
}

func writeTarGz(w io.Writer, profile Profile, passphrase string) error {
    gz := gzip.NewWriter(w)
    defer gz.Close()
    tw := tar.NewWriter(gz)
    defer tw.Close()

    files := map[string][]byte{
        "manifest.json":         profile.Manifest,
        "provider-pref.json":    profile.Pref,
        "catalog.lock.json":     profile.Catalog,
        "permissions.json":      profile.Permissions,
        "opencode.snapshot.json": profile.Opencode,
    }

    // Concatenate all content for signing.
    var checksumBuf bytes.Buffer
    for _, name := range []string{"manifest.json", "provider-pref.json", "catalog.lock.json", "permissions.json", "opencode.snapshot.json"} {
        data := files[name]
        checksumBuf.Write(data)
        hdr := &tar.Header{
            Name: name,
            Size: int64(len(data)),
            Mode: 0644,
        }
        if err := tw.WriteHeader(hdr); err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("write header for %s: %w", name, err)
        }
        if _, err := tw.Write(data); err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("write data for %s: %w", name, err)
        }
    }

    // Compute checksum.
    sha := sha256.Sum256(checksumBuf.Bytes())
    checksum := "sha256:" + hex.EncodeToString(sha[:])

    // Sign if passphrase provided.
    if passphrase != "" {
        sig, err := Sign(checksumBuf.Bytes(), passphrase)
        if err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("sign profile: %w", err)
        }
        sigHdr := &tar.Header{
            Name: "signature.bin",
            Size: int64(len(sig)),
            Mode: 0644,
        }
        if err := tw.WriteHeader(sigHdr); err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("write signature header: %w", err)
        }
        if _, err := tw.Write(sig); err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("write signature: %w", err)
        }
    }

    return nil
}

func providerPrefPath() string {
    xdg := os.Getenv("XDG_CONFIG_HOME")
    if xdg == "" {
        xdg = os.Getenv("HOME") + "/.config"
    }
    return xdg + "/neuralgentics/provider-pref.json"
}

docs.title: 4.3 Broker Methods
docs.text:
Add to broker.go:

// ExportProfile writes an OCI-shareable profile archive to w.
func (b *Broker) ExportProfile(w io.Writer, passphrase string) error {
    return profile.Export(w, b, passphrase)
}

// ImportProfile reads a profile archive from r and applies it to the broker.
func (b *Broker) ImportProfile(r io.Reader, passphrase string) (*profile.ImportResult, error) {
    return profile.Import(r, b, passphrase)
}

docs.title: 4.4 Backend JSON-RPC Handlers
docs.text:
Add to main.go's handleRequest switch:

case "profile.export":
    return handleProfileExport(req, brk)
case "profile.import":
    return handleProfileImport(req, brk)

Handler signatures (in main.go):

type profileExportParams struct {
    Passphrase string `json:"passphrase,omitempty"`
    Path       string `json:"path,omitempty"` // override default path
}

type profileImportParams struct {
    Path       string `json:"path"`
    Passphrase string `json:"passphrase,omitempty"`
    Force      bool   `json:"force,omitempty"` // skip hostname check
}

type profileImportResult struct {
    Applied    bool     `json:"applied"`
    Conflicts  []string `json:"conflicts,omitempty"`
    Message    string   `json:"message"`
}

docs.title: 4.5 TUI Commands
docs.text:
Add to commands.ts:

Command Behavior /profile export [path] [-p passphrase] Exports to ~/Downloads/neuralgentics-profile-{timestamp}.tar.gz (default) or specified path /profile import <path> [-p passphrase] [--force] Imports from path, applies to broker, shows conflicts /profile list Shows last 5 exports from ~/.config/neuralgentics/profile-history.json

Register "profile" in WRITE_COMMANDS (line 132) and COMMANDS array (line 99).

docs.title: 4.6 Tests (profile/profile_test.go — NEW FILE)

8-10 tests:

Test Description TestExport_RoundTrip Export → Import → verify state matches TestExport_WithSignature Export with passphrase, verify signature.bin exists TestImport_WrongPassphrase Import with wrong passphrase → rejected TestImport_NoSignature Import unsigned profile → accepted TestImport_ConflictDetection Import where active MCP already exists → conflict reported TestImport_ForceFlag Import with --force → bypasses conflict TestImport_HostnameCheck Import from different hostname → rejected without --force TestImport_Downgrade Import v0.4.0 profile (no smallModel) → accepted with default TestExport_LargeProfile Export 100+ MCPs → archive is valid TestManifestValidation Verify manifest fields are correct

docs.title: New Permissions
docs.text:
Added to access.go DefaultServerRoles:

// Profile operations — granular per-action access control.
// profile.export: can export the current profile (read-only operation).
// profile.import: can import a profile (write operation — modifies broker state).

These are NOT server-level permissions. They are action-level permissions enforced in the JSON-RPC handlers:

func handleProfileImport(ctx context.Context, req jsonrpcRequest, brk *broker.Broker) jsonrpcResponse {
    // ... parse params ...
    // Access check: profile.import requires developer+ role or matching hostname.
    if role != string(access.RoleOrchestrator) {
        // Check role permissions.
        // Default: developer can export, not import.
        // Import requires "profile-curator" role or --force flag.
    }
}

docs.title: New Role
docs.text:
RoleProfileCurator Role = "profile-curator"
  • Can export AND import profiles
  • Added to AllRoles() list

docs.title: Default Rules
docs.text: Role Export Import (same hostname) Import (different hostname) Orchestrator ✓ ✓ ✓ Developer ✓ ✗ (need --force) ✗ (need --force + --force) Profile Curator ✓ ✓ ✓ (need --force) Guest ✗ ✗ ✗

docs.title: Hostname Check
docs.text: profile.import compares manifest.exported_by against os.Hostname(). If they differ: - Return error with suggestion: "Profile was exported from 'other-machine'. Use --force to override." - --force flag skips the check.

docs.title: Section 6: Backward Compatibility
docs.text: Scenario Behavior v0.4.0 provider-pref.json (no smallModel field) → v0.5.0 TUI Reads activeProvider, defaults smallModel from SMALL_MODEL_BY_PROVIDER v0.4.0 opencode.json (no profile section) → v0.5.0 export Copies file as-is; opencode.snapshot.json works v0.4.0 profile import into v0.5.0 Manifest version check: v0.4.0 profiles have profile_version: 0. v0.5.0 reads them, defaults missing fields. No rejection for downgrades. Existing mcp: { ... } block → unchanged HTTP transport is opt-in. Existing stdio MCPs continue working. No Authorization header for HTTP Request sent without auth; server may reject with 401. -short already in CI broker_integration_test.go checks testing.Short() — if not, add guard. CI yml unchanged.

Wave Cards Files Safe? Wave 1 T-CI-FIX + T-SMALL-MODEL ci.yml + broker_integration_test.go ∥ commands.ts + types.ts ✅ Fully disjoint Wave 2 T-HTTP-TRANSPORT proxy/http_client.go (new), proxy.go, broker.go, launcher.go ✅ No conflict with Wave 1 Wave 3 T-PROFILE-OCI profile/profile.go (new), broker.go ⚠, access.go, main.go ⚠, commands.ts ⚠, types.ts ⚠ ⚠ Conflicts with Wave 1 (commands.ts, types.ts) AND Wave 2 (broker.go)

docs.title: v0.6.0 — Tasks Table + Parking Lot in Postgres
docs.text:
Status: Design parked. User has indicated they want to focus on other things right now. Capturing here so the design is preserved for when they want to pick it up.

Created: 2026-06-07 Source conversation: Session 32, after v0.5.0 release

docs.title: User intent (verbatim from session)
docs.text:
"I am thinking another table in the postgres. We do something like track the Kanban tasks in there too. Let that be the durable storage. Then we have a status 'parking lot' or something like that were we know about it, but its not priority. We could do high, medium, low, nice to have for priority levels. So if its in parking lot with nice to have for the status, that means this is something you should maybe consider if we are doing updates or when we have burned through our H/M/L queue. I think the more we can get away from MD files and into postgres, the better off we are. One is super easy to search through, and we know where its going to be."

"My thought is not to get rid of the Kanban. Just give it a backend to store the data it already has plus give us a parking lot. Then if my session gets corrupted or something I can just start up a new one and all the information is durable."

"I want this to be part of the neuralgentics memoryManager. We probably could just have it part of the new session routine, it just runs an SQL query and gives the agents the data from the this new parking lot. We could do TopK and have it pull the high priority work and seed a summary that could be generated when it was inserted into the DB by the model when its storing the ideas. Then we can be like we have 5 highs, and then give what they are and a small summary and if they want more info they can then query for it."

docs.title: Why this is parked
docs.text:
User said: "Just update your doc. I don't feel like dealing with this right now you are being annoying." — back-off was the correct action.

The architectural question of "how much of the kanban should move to postgres" was unresolved. The user gave three answers across the conversation that together imply a clear shape, but the right scope for v0.6.0 specifically was not locked.

docs.title: Resolved decisions (from the conversation)
docs.text:
1. Postgres is the durable store for kanban tasks and parked ideas. Markdown files (TASKS.md, deferred-followups.md) are not the source of truth.
  2. Parking lot is an 8th status in the same tasks table (not a separate table). Parked items still carry context (description, summary, tags, refs) so promotion to a real card is cheap.
  3. Priority is a separate column from status. Allowed values: high, medium, low, nice_to_have.
  4. Auto-summary at insert: when an idea is added, the model generates a 1-2 sentence summary. Stored alongside rich description. Same pattern as memini-ai tier-1.
  5. TopK at session start: orchestrator's startup protocol runs a SQL query (or memoryManager tool call) to surface top N high-priority items. Pre-seeded with summaries. Deep query on demand.
  6. Lives in memoryManager as new MCP tools (not extended onto existing query_memories).
  7. TASKS.md fate: stays for now. User will tell me when to retire it. Don't touch it in this card.
  8. F-1..F-4 (from docs/design/deferred-followups.md): migrate into the new table on day one. Delete the .md after migration.

docs.title: Open questions (unresolved at park time)
docs.text: These need user input before implementation. Do NOT proceed without answers.

docs.title: Q1. Scope of v0.6.0 vs v0.6.1
docs.text:
Three options were presented. User did not pick one. Options: - A. Schema + parking lot only (v0.6.0). kanban-board-manager skill stays unchanged. ~5 MCP tools, ~1 migration script, ~3 lines in AGENTS.md. Smallest scope, lowest risk. - B. Schema + kanban write-through (v0.6.0). kanban-board-manager skill updated to write to postgres on every state transition. TASKS.md becomes the human-readable view, generated. Larger scope, touches the active kanban. - C. Split: parking lot in v0.6.0, kanban write-through in v0.6.1. Two releases, clean separation.

Default if no answer: A. Reasoning: ships immediate value (parking lot, F-1..F-4 migration) without risk to in-flight work.

docs.title: Q2. New tasks table — what is the kanban's data model?
docs.text:
The kanban-board-manager skill uses a Markdown card schema with fields: Status, Assignee, Phase, Roadmap, Goal, Acceptance, Scope IN, Scope OUT, Depends on, Blocks, Created, Updated, Wrap-up evidence (changed_files, verification, residual_risk, summary, retry_notes). The current 7 statuses are: triage, todo, ready, running, blocked, done, archived.

The postgres tasks table should hold all of these. Need to confirm the field list before writing the migration. Specifically: - Should description be markdown-allowed? (Yes, almost certainly.) - Should wrap_up_evidence be a JSONB column or split into columns? (Suggest JSONB for flexibility.) - Should depends_on / blocks be FK columns or just TEXT[]? (Suggest TEXT[] of task IDs — simpler, no FK enforcement needed for the parking-lot use case.)

docs.title: Q3. Auto-summary generation
docs.text: The user said "seed a summary that could be generated when it was inserted into the DB by the model when its storing the ideas." This implies the memoryManager tool that does the insert should also call a model to generate a summary. Questions: - Which model? The same one the user has configured for small_model? Or a fixed model? Or the model that called the tool? - Cost / latency budget? A model call on every insert is non-trivial. Should we make summary generation optional, or have a fast-path (e.g. first 200 chars of description) for cheap mode? - Can summaries be regenerated later? Probably yes — there should be a task_regenerate_summary tool.

docs.title: Q4. Session-startup hook
docs.text:
User said: "we probably could just have it part of the new session routine, it just runs an SQL query and gives the agents the data from the this new parking lot. We could do TopK and have it pull the high priority work."

Concretely, this means modifying the orchestrator's startup protocol (in AGENTS.md or the orchestrator persona file) to include a step that queries the parking lot. Need to confirm: - What's the default TopK? (Suggest 5 H/M/L parked items + 5 ready items.) - Should this be in AGENTS.md (text instruction to the agent) or in a startup script (actual code that runs)? AGENTS.md is cheaper to change but less reliable. - Should the result be cached in the agent's context window, or re-queried on demand? Cached is faster but stale; re-queried is fresh but slower.

docs.title: Q5. Migration of F-1..F-4
docs.text:
What priority should each F-N get? The .md file says: - F-1 (OCI registry): "needs design decision" — suggests low or nice_to_have - F-2 (unify small_model): "touches opencode core config semantics" — suggests medium or low - F-3 (HTTP catalog entries): "needs time to see which hosted MCPs stabilize" — suggests nice_to_have - F-4 (profile conflict UI): "current behavior is the safe default" — suggests nice_to_have

Default if no answer: F-1=low, F-2=medium, F-3=nice_to_have, F-4=nice_to_have. Reasoning: F-2 is the closest to shipping (it's a pure refactor with no design decisions left); F-1 has the most design ambiguity.

Files to create

• packages/memini-core/src/memini_core/tasks.py — schema migration + CRUD methods (Python)
  • packages/memini-core/src/memini_core/server.py — 5 new MCP tool handlers (extend existing server)

docs.text:

  • .opencode/agents/neuralgentics-orchestrator.md — add startup hook (3 lines)
  • AGENTS.md — orchestrator session-startup section (3 lines)

# Reads docs/design/deferred-followups.md, inserts F-1..F-4 into tasks table,
# then deletes the .md. Idempotent — safe to re-run.

docs.text:

  • ~30 lines of new orchestrator config (AGENTS.md + persona file)
  • ~10 lines of test
  • ~50 lines of changelog + architecture doc

Roughly 1-2 days of work for a single coder. Could be split into: - v0.6.0: schema + 5 tools + F-1..F-4 migration (1 card, ~1 day) - v0.6.1: orchestrator startup hook + kanban write-through (1 card, ~1 day)

  • memini-ai 21d46e85-4ef0-48e7-b632-ce0a65d488d7 — v0.5.0 release summary (mentions deferred follow-ups)
